Chief Complaint:
Locally advanced gastric carcinoma
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Cystoscopy for foley catheter placement; Laparoscopy with 
biopsy; Gastroscopy

 
History of Present Illness:
Mr. [DEID] is a [DEID] year old male with locally advanced gastric 
cancer (stage II [T3N2]) who arrives to [DEID] for 
curative-intent surgical resection after completion of induction 
chemotherapy. 

He was started on neoadjuvant chemotherapy with FLOT4 on
[DEID]. Overall he tolerated the chemotherapy well without any 
significant side effects. However, last
month he developed right upper extremity edema and was noted to
have a thrombosis in the R SVC. He was started on Lovenox 1 
mg/kg
BID, which he is compliant with. Denies any fever, nausea,
emesis, chills, weight loss, melena, hematochezia or hematuria. 

He comes after recent repeat staging ([DEID]) with torso CT
scan demonstrating no evidence of disease. He is now now taken
to the operating room for minimally invasive and possibly open
radical distal gastrectomy with lymphadenectomy. The risks
and benefits of surgery have been described with the patient
in detail and are documented by Dr. [DEID] in a separate
note.
 
Past Medical History:
Past Medical History: Prostate cancer, Thyroid nodule,
Hypothyroid, GERD mild, Diverticulosis on colonoscopy [DEID]
anemia iron def. anemia which resolved and no workup

Past Surgical History: Prostate cancer external beam [DEID],
Achilles tendon repair [DEID], Shattered right tibia and fibula,
Tonsillectomy age [DEID].
 
Social History:
[DEID]

Father died at [DEID] from Lymphoma. Mother died at [DEID] with type II 
DM, Dementia.

 Temp: 98.1 (Tm 98.9), LABTEST BP: 116 /73 (108-118/59-77), HR: 79 
(77-86), RR: 18 ([DEID]), O2 LABTEST sat: 99 % (97-99), O2 delivery: Ra 
GEN: A&Ox3, NAD, resting comfortably 
HEENT: NCAT, EOMI, sclera anicteric

PULM: no respiratory distress
ABD: soft, NT, ND, no rebound or guarding 
EXT: warm, well-perfused, no edema 
PSYCH: normal insight, memory, and mood
WOUND(S): Incision c/d/i
 
Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. [DEID] is a [DEID] year old Male who presented on [DEID] for 
a planned minimally invasive and possibly open radical distal 
gastrectomy with lymphadenectomy for locally advanced gastric 
carcinoma after chemotherapy. After placement of the Right upper 
quadrant port, insufflation revealed the right upper quadrant 
port to be penetrating the omentum. It appeared to miss the 
transverse mesocolon as well as the colon itself. During the 
surgery, there were visually obvious peritoneal deposits on all 
peritoneal surfaces in all four quadrants, which demonstrated 
undetected carcinomatosis, poorly -differentiated adenocarcinoma 
involving the peritoneum. For this reason, the procedure was 
then aborted. Post-operatively the patient was taken to the PACU 
until stable and then transferred to the wards until stable to 
go home. 

#NEURO: The patient was alert and oriented throughout 
hospitalization; pain was initially managed with dilaudid PCA 
which he was not using and then transitioned to tramadol which 
he got only one dose. Pain was very well controlled.

#CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. 

#PULMONARY: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization. 

#GI/GU/FEN: Before the procedure started, OR nurse was unable to 
pass Foley after attempt with standard and coude type. PA 
[DEID], with usual sterile technique, re-attempted foley 
placement after 10cc urojet application with [DEID] and [DEID] 
coude-type catheters but was also unable to get passed the 
prostate. Urology was consulted and they performed a flexible 
cystoscope demonstrating a normal urethra. Using a flexible 
guidewire, cystoscope was advanced into the bladder. The scope 
was withdrawn and a [DEID] council was advanced over the wire, 
passed the prostate and into the bladder. The patient was 
discharged with the Foley in place with instructions to 
follow-up with urology as outpatient in 5 to 7 days for a 
voiding trial. No antibiotics were administered. The patient was 
tolerating a regular diet prior to discharge. 

#ID: The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs 
of infection, of which there were none. 

#HEME: Patient received BID SQH for DVT prophylaxis, in addition 
to encouraging early ambulation and Venodyne compression 
devices. On POD1 the patient was restarted on his home 
enoxaparin before discharge.

#TRANSITIONAL ISSUES
--------------------

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating diet as 
above per oral, ambulating, and pain was well controlled. The 
patient was discharged home with the Foley catheter with 
appropriate teaching for care. The patient received discharge 
teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding 
verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. [DEID],

It was a pleasure taking care of you here at [DEID] 
[DEID]. You were admitted to our hospital for 
gastric cancer. You had an attempted Robot-assisted laparoscopic 
partial gastrectomy and gastroscopy on [DEID] without 
complications. You tolerated the procedure well and are 
ambulating, stooling, tolerating a regular diet, and your pain 
is controlled by pain medications by mouth. You are now ready to 
be discharged to home. Please follow the recommendations below 
to ensure a speedy and uneventful recovery. 

ACTIVITY:
- Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
- You may climb stairs. You should continue to walk several 
times a day. 
- You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until 
you see your surgeon at your next visit. 
- You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable. 
Slowly increase your activity back to your baseline as 
tolerated.
- Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common 
sense and go slowly at first. 
- No heavy lifting (10 pounds or more) until cleared by your 
surgeon, usually about 6 weeks. 
- You may resume sexual activity unless your doctor has told you 
otherwise.

HOW YOU MAY FEEL: 
- You may feel weak or "washed out" for 6 weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
- You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during the surgery.

YOUR BOWELS:
- Constipation is a common side effect of narcotic pain medicine 
such as oxycodone. If needed, you may take a stool softener 
(such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk 
of magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
- If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain 
moving the bowels, call your surgeon.
- After some operations, diarrhea can occur. If you get 
diarrhea, don't take anti-diarrhea medicines. Drink plenty of 
fluids and see if it goes away. If it does not go away, or is 
severe and you feel ill, please call your surgeon.

PAIN MANAGEMENT:
- You are being discharged with a prescription for **oxycodone 
for pain control. You may take Tylenol as directed, not to 
exceed 3500mg in 24 hours. Take regularly for a few days after 
surgery but you may skip a dose or increase time between doses 
if you are not having pain until you no longer need it. You may 
take the oxycodone for moderate and severe pain not controlled 
by the Tylenol. You may take a stool softener while on narcotics 
to help prevent the constipation that they may cause. Slowly 
wean off these medications as tolerated. 
- Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain 
is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.

If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon: 
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- chest pain, pressure, squeezing, or tightness
- cough, shortness of breath, wheezing
- pain that is getting worse over time or pain with fever
- shaking chills, fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
- nausea and vomiting, inability to tolerate fluids, food, or 
your medications
- if you are getting dehydrated (dry mouth, rapid heart beat, 
feeling dizzy or faint especially while standing)
-any change in your symptoms or any symptoms that concern you

Additional: 
*- pain that is getting worse over time, or going to your chest 
or back
*- urinary: burning or blood in your urine or the inability to 
urinate

MEDICATIONS:
- Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just 
as you did before, unless you have been told differently.
- If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not 
to take, please call your surgeon. 

WOUND CARE: 
- dressing removal:
- You may remove your dressings tomorrow [DEID] and shower that 
same day with any bandage strips that may be covering your 
wound. Do not scrub and do not soak or swim, and pat the 
incision dry. If you have steri strips, they will fall off by 
themselves in [DEID] weeks. If any are still on in two weeks and 
the edges are curling up, you may carefully peel them off.
 Do not take baths, soak, or swim for 6 weeks after surgery 
unless told otherwise by your surgical team. 
-Notify your surgeon is you notice abnormal (foul smelling, 
bloody, pus, etc) or increased drainage from your incision site, 
opening of your incision, or increased pain or bruising. Watch 
for signs of infection such as redness, streaking of your skin, 
swelling, increased pain, or increased drainage. 

Please call with any questions or concerns. Thank you for 
allowing us to participate in your care. We hope you have a 
quick return to your usual life and activities.

Home with [DEID]:
You had a Foley catheter in your bladder placed by urology on 
the day of your surgery after difficulty trying to place it.
You will keep the catheter until your appointment with Urology 
in 5 days (please call the number below to schedule your 
appointment), who will decide if you need it longer or attempt 
to remove it and see if you are able to void.
 Empty the bag as needed and as shown to you by nursing staff. 
You will be given a leg bag before your discharge, that you may 
use for short trips. This is a smaller bag that straps to your 
leg, to take home and wear if you are traveling outside your 
home. This holds a smaller amount than the bag you have now, so 
it needs to be emptied more often. Some people find it easier to 
use the larger bad when they are at home or carry it with them. 

-- Your [DEID] Care Team

Brief Hospital Course: Mr. [DEID] was admitted to the [DEID] on [DEID] for surgical management of his aortic valve and coronary artery disease. The patient was neurologically intact and hemodynamically stable, weaned from inotropic and vasopressor support. Beta blocker was initiated and the patient was gently diuresed toward the preoperative weight. The patient was transferred to the telemetry floor for further recovery. Chest tubes and pacing wires were discontinued without complication. Events: He developed post-operative atrial fibrillation and was started on amiodarone and Coumadin. He developed acute kidney injury on underlying chronic kidney disease. Urine output decreased and he returned to [DEID] for closer monitoring. He was treated for hyperkalemia with Kayexalte. Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the orthopaedic surgery service and 
was taken to the operating room for above described procedure. 
Please see separately dictated operative report for details. The 
surgery was uncomplicated and the patient tolerated the 
procedure well. Patient received perioperative IV antibiotics.

Otherwise, pain was initially controlled with a PCA followed by 
a transition to oral pain medications on POD#1. The patient 
received Aspirin 325mg for DVT prophylaxis starting on the 
morning of POD#1. The patient was seen daily by physical 
therapy. 

At the time of discharge the patient was tolerating a regular 
diet and feeling well. The patient was afebrile with stable 
vital signs. The patient's pain was adequately controlled on 
an oral regimen. The operative extremity was neurovascularly 
intact. The patient is non-weight-bearing on the LUE

condition with prescriptions for oxycontin and oxycodone

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital from ACS clinic for an abscess 
along your right flank after being treated for intra-abdominal 
abscess requiring percutaneous drainage. Imaging performed at 
an outside hospital revealed a persistent intra-abdominal 
abscess and stones. Therefore, we recommended admitting you to 
the hospital for abscess drainage in the operating room. On 
[DEID] you underwent abdominal exploration and drainage of your 
abscess. We gave you antibiotics for your infection. Your 
bowel function has returned and you have resumed a regular diet. 
Please follow up in [DEID] clinic. Your staples will be removed at 
that time.

ACTIVITY:
Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
You may climb stairs.
You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until you 
see your surgeon at your next visit.
Don't lift more than [DEID] lbs for [DEID] weeks. (This is about the 
weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to 
lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable.
You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common 
sense and go slowly at first.

HOW YOU MAY FEEL:
You may feel weak or "washed out" for 6 weeks. You might want to 
nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. You 
might feel somewhat depressed.
You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem 
unappealing.
All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.

YOUR INCISION:
Your incision may be slightly red around the staples. This is 
normal.
You may gently wash away dried material around your incision. 
It is normal to feel a firm ridge along the incision. This will 
go away.
Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid staining 
your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, please call 
your surgeon.
You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
Ove the next [DEID] months, your incision will fade and become 
less prominent.

Please change the dry dressing daily over where your JP drain 
was inserted and the dressing in the incision site daily. 

YOUR BOWELS:
Constipation is a common side effect of medicine such as 
Percocet or codeine. If needed, you may take a stool softener 
(such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk 
of magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain moving 
the bowels, call your surgeon.

PAIN MANAGEMENT:
It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness".
Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain is 
getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
You will receive a prescription from your surgeon for pain 
medicine to take by mouth.
You can take ibuprofen for pain as needed and percocet for 
severe pain. Please do not drive when taking percocet. You can 
take senna while taking percocet as it may be constipating.
It is important to take this medicine as directed. Do not take 
it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take more medicine at 
one time than prescribed.
Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.

If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain

MEDICATIONS:
Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just as 
you did before, unless you have been told differently.
Please continue taking augmentin, the antibiotic you took in the 
hospital, until [DEID].
If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not to 
take, please call your surgeon.

physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.
 
2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills. 
 
3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
 
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as Colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect. Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled. These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house. Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.
 
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.
 
6. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment.
 
7. SWELLING: Ice the operative joint 20 minutes at a time, 
especially after activity or physical therapy. Do not place ice 
directly on the skin. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal 
anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs such as Celebrex, 
ibuprofen, Advil, Aleve, Motrin, naproxen etc) until cleared by 
your physician.

8. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your Xarelto 10mg daily with 
food for four (4) weeks to help prevent deep vein thrombosis 
(blood clots). If you were taking Aspirin prior to your 
surgery, you can resume it at your home dose after you have 
completed Xarelto.

okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four (4) 
week checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound 
each day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air.

thick yellow drainage. Staples will be removed by the visiting 
nurse or rehab facility in two (2) weeks.

[DEID] year old female with polycystic kidney disease and 
hypertension admitted [DEID] with hypertensive 
emergency. Symptoms on presentation included headache, chest 
pressure, blurry vision, low back pain, and hematuria. Hospital 
course was as follows. Of note, patient left AMA because she 
needed to get home to her kids before the coming snow storm.

1. Hypertensive emergency: Identified in patient by markedly 
elevated blood pressure (systolic BP>200 in ED) in context of 
headache, chest pressure, and blurry vision. Patient reported 
not taking antihypertensives for past [DEID] months due to feeling 
they were affecting her weight. On arrival to the floor, patient 
still complained of chest pain and worsening headache; blurry 
vision was unchanged. On exam, patient had systolic blood 
pressure in 170s, no papilledema, a prominent S2 and positive 
S4, and lower extremity edema. She was given IV hydralazine and 
restarted slowly on her antihypertensive regimen. Initial blood 
pressure goal was 140-160. As the blood pressure came down, the 
patient's symptoms improved as well.
 CT head was normal. The patient had 4 sets of cardiac 
enzymes; all were negative. No arrhythmias were see on 
telemetry. EKG on day 2 of hospital course was concerning for 
new T wave changes in the inferior leads; patient was 
asymptomatic, and this resolved without intervention. The case 
was discussed briefly with the cardiology team. Patient was 
discharged with antihypertensive regimen including a 
beta-blocker and ACE inhibitor. She was also scheduled for an 
outpatient stress test and appointments with her PCP for blood 
pressure and electrolyte checks, cardiology, and ophthalmology. 
Given that patient's blood pressure was still elevated on day of 
discharge, patient was asked to stay as an inpatient; she was 
unable to do so due to her kids being home alone.

2. Hematuria: Differential diagnosis on admission included cyst 
rupture given history of [DEID], nephrolithiasis, UTI, and 
malignancy given urologic malignancy in father and brother 
(unclear which source in particular). No complex cysts or masses 
were seen on renal ultrasound. UA was without indication of 
active infection, and patient was without dysuria or signs of 
pyelonephritis on exam. Patient did complain of pain in RUQ/RLQ 
without radiation to groin. Both renal and urology were 
consulted and requested further workup; urine cytology was sent, 
and on day of discharge patient had MR urogram for further 
evaluation. No masses were seen. On discharge, hematocrit was 
stable at 29; this was down from 33 at admission. Most likely 
diagnosis is [DEID] cyst rupture. Patient was scheduled for close 
follow-up with renal and urology, and asked to return to the 
emergency room if she continues to have worsening hematuria.

3. Polycystic kidney disease: Patient has known involvement of 
liver as well. This is complicated by chronic kidney disease. On 
review of OMR, patient's creatinine has been slowly rising since 
[DEID]. Patient was scheduled for follow-up with renal as above.

Brief Hospital Course:
[DEID] year-old female presented on [DEID] with chest pain and PNA. 
She was started on levaquin for her PNA and she underwent 
cardiology evaluation for elevated troponins and EKG changes. 
She was diagnosed with NSTEMI. Therefore, she underwent cardiac 
catheterization and balloon angioplasty of her RCA on [DEID]. 
Patient's post-cath course includes being placed on a heparin 
gtt for atrial fibrillation and stable conservative management 
of a right groin hematoma. 

On post-catheterization day #5, the patient went into rapid 
atrial fibrillation in the early AM. Her HR was in the 150s and 
SBP in low [DEID]. Her heart rate was converted back to sinus with 
Digoxin and her SBP was now in 100s. However, 30 minutes after 
the patient went into Afib, she complained of severe abdominal 
pain in her right lower quadrant. General surgery consult was 
called a little later that morning, as there was concern that 
the patient displayed peritoneal signs. The general surgery 
team evaluated the patient 20 minutes after administration of 
prochlorperazine for nausea, and thus she was slightly sedated. 
However, she did complain of abdominal pain in mid-abdomen. She 
had not been passing flatus for the previous 36 hours, and she 
had one episode of vomiting earlier in the AM. A CT scan of 
the abdomen/pelvis was ordered on the patient and showed right 
colon and cecum with pneumatosis, areas of portal venous gas 
seen in liver, and air in veins running through iliac fossa that 
were likely branches of the portal vein. The patient and her 
family members were immediately contacted with the gravity of 
the diagnosis - the patient likely had ischemic injury to her 
colon either from a thrombus to a branch of the SMA or she had 
ischemic colon from a low flow state that was a consequence of 
her atrial fibrillation. After thorough discussion between Dr. 
[DEID] patient, and her family members reviewing all the 
risks and benefits of surgery, the patient was taken emergently 
to the operating room.

Patient underwent exploratory laparotomy on [DEID]. Findings 
included toxic megacolon and ischemic injury extending from the 
right colon through the transverse colon. The patient underwent 
a subtotal colectomy and end ileostomy. Because the patient's 
case occurred late in the evening on [DEID], she was kept 
intubated postoperatively. 

The patient's early postoperative course was marked by rapid 
atrial fibrillation with RVR. During the first 24 hours 
postoperative, she dropped her pressures to systolic of 50 and 
required cardioversion and adenosine injections. She was 
started on an esmolol gtt to control her atrial fibrillation, 
and this was then changed over to an amiodarone gtt. The 
patient was transfused one unit of packed red blood cells and 
was temporarily placed on a dobutamine gtt. The patient was 
evaluated by cardiology, who recommended that she placed back 
onto an amiodarone gtt. 

The patient underwent an ECHO cardiogram on POD 2 that revealed 
normal LV and RV function and an EF of 55-60%. The patient 
also had a leukocytosis and an elevation of her LFTs from POD4 
through POD7. Concern was raised as to whether the patient 
could have acute cholecystitis. However, she underwent a right 
upper quadrant ultrasound on POD5 that showed patient had no 
evidence of acute cholecystitis, and rather she may have an 
element of shock liver. The patient's LFTs and WBC eventually 
normalized. The patient was on and off neosynephrine gtt during 
this time period, however this was thought to be due to her 
slightly lower blood pressure as a consequence of propofol - the 
patient was intubated during this time period and remained on 
propofol. She was started on tube feeds and this was tolerated 
to goal.

Once the patient's leukocytosis and LFTs improved, the patient 
was extubated on POD 10. She had slight tachypnea. She was 
given lasix to remove excess fluid. However, the patient tired 
out on POD11 and she was re-intubated. CXR post-intubated showed 
a large right pleural effusion. The patient underwent 
thoracentesis by the SICU service and 900 cc of transudative 
pleural fluid was removed. However, post-thoracentesis CXR 
showed a right basilar pneumothorax. Patient therefore had a 
right pigtail tube placed in the right chest by IP. 

Between POD 11 and POD 16, the patient remained intubated and 
still on-and-off the neosynephrine gtt. After careful 
discussion with the patient's family regarding the patient's 
unlikelihood of weaning off the vent, the family consented to 
having the patient undergo a tracheostomy and PEG placement. 
The patient underwent PEG and trach on [DEID]. The pigtail 
was removed on POD16 ([DEID]). The patient did very well with 
tracheostomy in place and was able to wean off the vent and onto 
trach mask by POD 17. The patient was also able to tolerate TF 
at goal on the PEG. 

The patient was evaluated once more cardiology on the day before 
discharge. As she had been off the neosynephrine gtt since 
POD15, they recommended the patient remain on enteral amiodarone 
and lopressor for control of her heart rate/rhythm. 

Furthermore, the patient had remained on a heparin gtt from POD2 
through POD 19 for her atrial fibrillation. She was started on 
coumadin (3 mg qday) on POD17. 

At the time of discharge, the patient was tolerating trach mask 
and was off of pressors for greater than 72 hours. She was 
awake and following commands. She was tolerating goal tube 
feeds. The heparin gtt was discontinued on POD19 (the day of

Discharge Instructions:
You have had an abdominal operation. This sheet goes over some 
questions and concerns you or your family may have. If you have 
additional questions, or [DEID] understand something about your 
operation, please call your surgeon.

ACTIVITY:
Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
You may climb stairs.
You may go outside. But avoid traveling long distances until you 
see your surgeon at your next visit.
[DEID] lift more than [DEID] pounds for 6 weeks. (This is about 
the weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies 
to lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable. 
You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common 
sense and go slowly at first. 
You may resume sexual activity unless your doctor has told you 
otherwise.

HOW YOU MAY FEEL:
You may feel weak or “washed out” for 6 weeks. You might want to 
nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you. 
You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. You 
might feel somewhat depressed.
You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem 
unappealing.
All these feelings and reactions are normal and should go away 
in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.

YOUR INCISION:
Your incision may be slightly red around the stitches or 
staples. This is normal.
You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
Do not remove steri-strips for 2 weeks. (These are the thin 
paper strips that might be on your incision.) But if they fall 
off before that, it’s OK.
It is normal to feel a firm ridge along the incision. This will 
go away.
Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid staining 
your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, please call 
your surgeon.
You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming. 
Over the next [DEID] months, your incision will fade and become 
less prominent.

YOUR BOWELS:
Constipation is a common side effect of medicine such as 
Percocet or codeine. If needed, you may take a stool softener 
(such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as Milk 
of Magnesia, 1 tablespoon) twice a day. You can get both of 
these medicines without a prescription.
If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain moving 
the bowels, call your surgeon.
After some operations, diarrhea can occur. If you get diarrhea, 
[DEID] take anti-diarrhea medicines. Drink plenty of fluids and 
see if it goes away. If it does not go away, or is severe and 
you feel ill, please call your surgeon.

PAIN MANAGEMENT:
It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as “soreness.”
Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain is 
getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
You will receive a prescription from your surgeon for pain 
medicine to take by mouth. It is important you take this 
medicine as directed. Do not take it more frequently than 
prescribed. Do not take more medicine at one time than 
prescribed.
Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please [DEID] take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said it is OK. 
If you are experiencing no pain, it is OK to skip a dose of pain 
medicine. 
To reduce pain, remember to exhale with any exertion or when you 
change positions. 
Remember to use your “cough pillow” for splinting when you cough 
or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
pain that is getting worse over time
pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain

MEDICATIONS:
Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just as 
you did before, unless you have been told differently. 
If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not to 
take, please call your surgeon.

DANGER SIGNS:
Please call your surgeon or go to the emergency room if you 
develop:
Worsening abdominal pain
Sharp or severe pain that lasts several hours 
Temperature of 101 degrees or higher 
Severe diarrhea
Vomiting
Redness around the incision that is spreading 
Increased swelling around the incision
Excessive bruising around the incision
Cloudy fluid coming from the wound
Bright red blood or foul smelling discharge coming from the 
wound
An increase in drainage from the wound

Discharge Instructions:
Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery
Lower Extremity Angioplasty/Stent Discharge Instructions

Medications:
•If instructed take Aspirin 325mg (enteric coated) once daily 
•If instructed, take Plavix (Clopidogrel) 75mg once daily
•Continue all other medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
•You make take Tylenol or prescribed pain medications for any 
post procedure pain or discomfort

What to expect when you go home:
It is normal to have slight swelling of the legs:
•Elevate your leg above the level of your heart (use [DEID] 
pillows or a recliner) every [DEID] hours throughout the day and at 
night
•Avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting without your 
legs elevated
•It is normal to feel tired and have a decreased appetite, your 
appetite will return with time 
•Drink plenty of fluids and eat small frequent meals
•It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
•To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
•When you go home, you may walk and go up and down stairs
•You may shower (let the soapy water run over groin incision, 
rinse and pat dry)
•Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing or 
band aid over the area that is draining, as needed
•No heavy lifting, pushing or pulling (greater than 5 lbs) for 
1 week (to allow groin puncture to heal)
•After 1 week, you may resume sexual activity
•After 1 week, gradually increase your activities and distance 
walked as you can tolerate
•No driving until you are no longer taking pain medications
•Call and schedule an appointment to be seen in [DEID] weeks for 
post procedure check and ultrasound 

What to report to office:
•Numbness, coldness or pain in lower extremities 
•Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
•New or increased drainage from incision or white, yellow or 
green drainage from incisions
•Bleeding from groin puncture site

SUDDEN, SEVERE BLEEDING OR SWELLING (Groin puncture site)
•Lie down, keep leg straight and have someone apply firm 
pressure to area for 10 minutes. If bleeding stops, call 
vascular office [DEID]. If bleeding does not stop, call 
[DEID] for transfer to closest Emergency Room.

PSYCHIATRIC
#) BIPOLAR D/O, MANIC EPISODE

The patient presented with the following signs/sx:
-pressured speech
-disorganized thoughts
-racing thoughts
-expansive mood
-bizarre/impulsive behavior

Patient was initially started on abilify and lorazepam for her 
acute mania. She initially refused the abilify, but did take it 
a few times. She felt that the lorazepam was too strong and 
after a few days, her standing dose (0.5mg BID) was changed to 
prn. Because she continued to be medication noncompliant, and 
the abilify long acting injectable is still new and not carried 
in the [DEID] pharmacy, she was switched to risperidone (2mg Qhs 
initially) with intention to eventually give her the risperdal 
consta shot because of her history of medication noncompliance. 
She took the risperdal very intermittantly, and complained of 
leg shaking, for which she was prescribed benztropine, which she 
also rarely agreed to take. 

Due to her long trajectory of med non-compliance and current 
refusal to accept treatment with no relief in symptoms, we 
decided to file for [DEID] commitment. Patient was educated 
about mood stabalizers and was offered lithium and depakote. On 
[DEID] patient agreed to try lithium and she was started on 300mg 
BID, which she tolerated well. Lithium was stopped in favor of 
risperdal (which can be given as a long acting injectable) after 
the patient was court committed on [DEID]. Patient was court 
committed on [DEID] and was given 25mg IM risperdal consta at that 
time. She was also ordered for risperdal po, which she 
initially refused and was given a 9.75mg aripiprazole IM 
injection. After receiving the IM aripiprazole, patient began to 
voluntarily take po risperdal. 

Patient received her second injection of risperdal on [DEID] and 
third injection on [DEID] prior to discharge. The patient 
reported feeling "jittery" and "restless" when receiving 
risperdal 6mg po; the dose was down-titrated and discontinued 
prior discharge. The patient's symptoms improved with lower 
doses of po riperdal and propranolol 10mg po t.i.d.

During the week prior to her discharge, the patient became acutely 
unstable during a family meeting where the goal was to discuss 
d/c plans and she started threatening to harm staff. She 
required emergency medication in the form of Abilify 9.75mg IM 
and Ativan 2mg IM. After that episoe she improved in terms of 
behavioral control and was much more appropriate. Patient's 
racing thoughts and pressured speech gradually improved, 
although she remained uninsightful as to her illness and did not 
believe that she required medication. As a result the treatment 
team decided to pursue guardianship of the patient.

GENERAL MEDICAL CONDITIONS
#) no acute medical needs on the unit. Patient's TSH and Free 
T4 were wnl when checked because patient had some homogenous 
thyromegally. Patient's PCP [DEID] was contacted and was 
happy that patient was getting treated for her bipolar disorder.

#) Health Maintenance
-Patient received the flu vaccine on [DEID].

PSYCHOSOCIAL
#) FAMILY CONTACT
The patient's mother presented for a family meeting on [DEID]. The 
mother visited about daily on the unit and often met with the 
team and patient to discuss plans. She was highly concerned 
for the patient's recent mania and her h/o stopping meds immediately 
upon d/c from hospitals in the past.

The mother was in favor of our decision to file for [DEID] due 
to the patient's lengthy h/o deterioration in the setting of 
lack of insight and med noncompliance.

Per the patient's mother, at the patient's baseline, she is not 
confrontational, grandiose, expansive, or dangerous. The patient is 
typically pleasant, organized and euthymic, but shows periods of 
decompensation throughout the year which have been increasing in 
frequency and severity.

Another family meeting was scheduled with the patient's father. At 
that time, the patient destabilized and required emergency chemical 
restraint after threatening staff. The following week, on 
[DEID], we had another meeting with the patient's mother and 
father. She tolerated that meeting much better and it was 
decided that the patient was appropriate for d/c to the community.

On [DEID] the team met once again with the patient and her 
father to review the d/c plan. Although the original plan was 
for the patient to attend partial, the team received notice from 
[DEID] insurance that the patient had no benefits for partial. She 
filed a form with NEU to reinstate her insurance via college, 
and the patient and her father agreed that they felt ready for d/c 
despite not being able to pay out of pocket for the partial. We 
encouraged the father to help the patient keep structure in her day, 
attend appts with her PCP, psychiatrist and therapist, and to 
connect with D[DEID] services as we arranged. The patient was given a 
courtesy 2 week script of meds. She was educated about warning 
signs of decompensation and instx to go to the nearest ED if 
signs occured. She and her father verbalized understanding the 
plan.

#) NEU COUNSELLING CENTER COLLATERAL
The team here did not know the patient very well, but was aware 
that the patient had been sleeping on sofas on the campus overnight 
without permission and that police had become involved in 
removing the patient from campus. The patient was placed on a leave 
of absence from school for the semester with a plan to resume 
classes in [DEID].

#) GROUPS/MILIEU
Ms. [DEID] did not regularly attend groups during the first half 
of her hospitalization. When she did attend groups, she was 
easily distractable and would routinely leave early. Despite 
this, she was social with peers in the Day Room and was 
typically pleasant with staff and other patients.

LEGAL STATUS
The patient initially was admitted on a [DEID] as she refused to 
sign a CV. She then agreed to sign a CV as her [DEID] was 
expiring and the team did not feel she was ready for discharge. 
She then promptly changed her mind and signed a 3-day notice.

We filed for [DEID] due to the patient's demonstrated lack of insight 
and inability to care for herself safely in the community.

[DEID].

The team filed for guardianship of this patient. Her parents 
were in support of this plan. 

RISK ASSESSMENT:
The patient is considered to be at a low immediate risk of harm 
and will be discharged into the community which is the least 
restrictive setting of care at this time.

During her admission, she was able to remain safe, engaged in no 
self-harmful acts, consistently denied suicidal 
thoughts/plans/intent, and expressed a strong desire to return 
home, continue her studies at [DEID], get a dog, resume 
daily workouts, and to eventually attend law school.

Despite the above, the patient has had a lengthy hospitalization 
for manic decompensation and this is her [DEID] hospitalization in 
the past year and a half. The patient has several static risk 
factors which increase her risk for self-harm in the future. 
These include; h/o recurrent noncompliance with medications, h/o 
repeatedly failing to follow up with outpatient treaters, h/o 
aggressiveness/behavioral dysregulation, and lacking the ability 
to self-soothe. In addition, her age demographic also elevates 
her risk.

Modifiable risk factors include; lack of outpatient treaters, 
lack of insight into her illness, and poor coping skills. We 
modified these by her [DEID] hospital stay, intense therapy 
and education about her illness, transition to Risperdal Consta 
injections (3 doses were given during the course of her 
hospitalization), establishment with DMH support, and the 
support of her family. The patient was also connected with 
outpatient care including psychiatry, and therapy. She has 
accepted treatment and agrees with the plan to attend a PHP and 
to follow up with DMH and she has longterm goals to complete 
college and attend law school.

Her protective factors include; intelligence, motivation for 
many future events, lack of suicidal ideation, no history of 
self-injurious behavior, large friend network, and support from 
her parents who are highly supportive of the patient getting 
well. 

PROGNOSIS
Ms. [DEID] has [DEID] strengths that can be maximized via 
therapy, daily structure, and medication management. Compliance 
with the aftercare plan will contribute to a good prognosis.

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital with acute appendicitis. You 
were taken to the operating room and had your appendix removed 
laparoscopically. You tolerated the procedure well and are now 
being discharged home to continue your recovery with the 
following instructions.
 
Please follow up in the Acute Care Surgery clinic at the 
appointment listed below.
 
ACTIVITY:
 
o Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
o You may climb stairs. 
o You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until 
you see your surgeon at your next visit.
o Don't lift more than [DEID] lbs for 4 weeks. (This is about 
the weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies 
to lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
o You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable.
o You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
 
HOW YOU MAY FEEL: 
o You may feel weak or "washed out" for a couple of weeks. You 
might want to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
o You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
o You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. 
You might feel somewhat depressed.
o You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem 
unappealing.
o All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.
 
YOUR INCISION:
o Tomorrow you may shower and remove the gauzes over your 
incisions. Under these dressing you have small plastic bandages 
called steri-strips. Do not remove steri-strips for 2 weeks. 
(These are the thin paper strips that might be on your 
incision.) But if they fall off before that that's okay).
o Your incisions may be slightly red around the stitches. This 
is normal.
o You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
o Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
o Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
o You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid 
staining your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, 
please call your surgeon.
o You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
 
YOUR BOWELS:
o Constipation is a common side effect of narcotic pain 
medications. If needed, you may take a stool softener (such as 
Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk of 
magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
o If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain 
moving the bowels, call your surgeon. 
 
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
o It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness". 
o Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain 
is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
o You will receive a prescription for pain medicine to take by 
mouth. It is important to take this medicine as directed. o Do 
not take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take more 
medicine at one time than prescribed.
o Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
o Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
o If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.
o Remember to use your "cough pillow" for splinting when you 
cough or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
 
MEDICATIONS:
Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just as 
you did before, unless you have been told differently.
If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not to 
take, please call your surgeon.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. [DEID] is a [DEID] year old male with a history of 
thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm s/p TEVAR ([DEID]) with partial 
left subclavian coverage s/p left carotid-subclavian bypass 
([DEID]). He was taken to the operating room on [DEID] and 
underwent completion open thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm 
repair. For details of the procedure, please see the surgeon's 
operative note. Intraoperatively, the patient lost both sensory 
and motor evoked potentials in the lower extremities. CSF was 
drained aggressively. Sensory potentials returned, but motor 
potentials were not able to be evoked for the remainder of the 
case. The patient was ultimately transferred to the ICU in 
critical condition. 

The patient had a long and complex post operative course 
including a prolonged ICU stay, spinal cord ischemia with lower 
extremity paralysis, respiratory failure requiring reintubation, 
and renal failure requiring CRRT and ultimately hemodialysis. 
These issues are detailed below by system. The patient was 
stable for transfer to the surgical floor on [DEID], and he 
continued to progress in his recovery but developed AFIIB RVR 
and hypotension with HD on [DEID] requiring transfer back to the 
ICU. His HR quickly normalized with amio and his taper was 
restarted, he was found to have another pneumonia and treated 
for that (detailed below). He continues to progress and he was 
finally deemed ready for discharge to acute rehab on [DEID].

Neuro: The patient developed spinal cord ischemia 
intraoperatively which persisted post operatively despite our 
efforts (CSF drainage, MAP goal > 80 per our neuroprotection 
rescue protocol). This ischemic insult unfortunately resulted in 
lower extremity paralysis. In addition, the patient developed 
hypoactive delirium during his ICU stay, which quickly resolved 
upon transfer to the surgical floor. He was depressed given the 
circumstances and was seen throughout his stay by Psychiatry who 
recommended continuing home sertraline 50mg daily and adding 
Ramelteon for insomnia. He was also started on low dose Ativan 
prn prior to HD for anxiety.

Cardiovascular: The patient's post operative course was 
complicated by atrial fibrillation with RVR. He was loaded with 
amiodarone, started on an infusion, and then transitioned to an 
oral amiodarone taper. Cardiology was consulted and recommended 
initiation of anticoagulation when surgically appropriate. 
Coumadin was started on [DEID] for a goal INR of [DEID]. He also had 
a persistent pressor requirement post operatively and was 
started on midodrine 20mg TID. His hypotension was not fluid 
responsive and was presumed to be secondary to spinal shock. His 
hypotension gradually improved. By the time he was transferred 
to the surgical floor, he was only requiring midodrine prior to 
hemodialysis. He was started on anticoagulation for his afib 
with warfarin and was on an amio taper. He will finish 200 BID 
on [DEID] and transition to 200 daily until he follows up with his 
outpatient cardiology. He is currently on metoprolol 25 TID 
which is held on HD days due to HD associated hypotension.

Pulmonary: The patient was extubated on POD12 but required 
reintubation shortly thereafter due to acute respiratory 
failure, presumably in the setting of aspiration. He completed a 
7 day course of cefepime/flagyl for aspiration pneumonia. He was 
extubated again on [DEID] and weaned to room air without issue. He 
developed recurrent pneumonia on [DEID] and was treated with a 10 
day course of Vanc/ Ceftaz. He is at very high risk for 
recurrent pneumonia given his weakness and the aneurysm sac 
compression of his left lung space. He was evaluated by IP for 
possible bronchial stent placement but determined to not be a 
candidate due to the radial force not being strong enough to 
withstand the compression. He will continue with aggressive 
pulmonary toilet and IS at rehab.

GI: The patient had a dobhoff tube placed and was started on 
tube feeds as soon as possible post operatively. Once extubated 
with improvement in his mental status, he was evaluated by 
speech and swallow who cleared him for a thickened diet and 
slowly advanced him to a regular diet. The dobhoff tube was 
removed in this setting. He was kept on strict aspiration 
precautions throughout admission. 

Renal: Patient developed acute renal failure post operatively 
and required initiation of CRRT in the ICU via a R IJ tunneled 
HD line. Once his hypotension improved, he was transitioned to 
intermittent hemodialysis. His renal funciton did demonstrate 
some possible signs of recovery as he has started making 300-400 
cc of urine daily. He was set up with an outpatient dialysis 
center at the time of discharge for ongoing treatment. He will 
also require evaluation for long term dialysis access (AV 
fistula/graft) as an outpatient if renal function does not 
return. 

Infectious disease: The patient completed a 7 day course of 
cefepime/flagyl for treatment of aspiration pneumonia during his 
ICU stay (described above). He then developed an leukocytosis 
with low grade fevers. Infectious workup revealed a likely UTI, 
so he was treated with a 7 day course of zosyn. He developed a 
second pneumonia detailed above and was on a 10 day course of 
vanc ceftaz which was completed [DEID].

Heme: In total this hospitalization he received 24 units of 
PRBCs, 18 units of FFP, 7 units of platelets, and 2 units of 
cryoprecipitate. He was initiated on Coumadin on [DEID] and INR on 
discharge was 1.6.

Transitional issues: 
He has follow up scheduled with Dr. [DEID] Dr. [DEID]. Please 
obtain CBC/BMP for follow-up. 
Please continue daily bladder scans and hemodialysis as needed. 
Please taper amiodarone on [DEID] to 200mg once daily until patient 
follows up with his cardiologist.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms [DEID] was admitted with right lower quadrant pain. We 
managed her for salpingitis, likely infectious in origin, 
although it remains unclear if this is purely an infectious 
salpingitis or related to her Crohn's disease. We started her 
on broad spectrum antibiotics with consequent improvement of 
admission leukocytosis and fevers. Her abdominal pain also 
significantly improved. We did obtain a pelvic MRI which did 
not reveal any communicating fistula between the right salpinx 
and the nearby inflamed small bowel. We held her humira given 
the possibility of active infection. She tolerated a transition 
to oral antibiotics (doxycycline and metronidazole which she 
will continue for an additional 12 days). She did continue to 
have thrombocytosis which may be related to the active infection 
or Crohns. She also passed a small amount of vaginal clots 
which resolved towards the end of her hospital stay. There was 
a question of whether there was a fluid collection associated 
with the salpingitis - pelvic MR support dilation of the right 
salpinx and associated fluid but no significant abscess - so 
drainage was performed. She was advised to continue on oral 
antibiotics and follow up with GI and Gynecology, who we are 
setting up follow appointments with. She knows to return if 
worsening pain or fevers. We also prescribed dilaudid and

your physician.

drainage, otherwise leave it open to air. Check wound regularly 
for signs of infection such as redness or thick yellow drainage.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient presented on [DEID] to the [DEID] ED for evaluation 
of prolonged nausea and inability to maintain adequate PO intake 
s/p laparoscopic cholecystectomy on [DEID] [DEID]. 
The patient has been worked up extensively for these symptoms 
prior to her cholecystectomy and there was no identifiable cause 
of her symptoms. She was also counseled pre-op that her 
cholecystectomy, for recurrent biliary colic, cholelithiasis, 
and biliary dyskinesia, was unlikely to alleviate her symptoms. 
Nonetheless, the patient's symptoms of nausea persisted in the 
post-operative period and the patient presented to the [DEID] ED 
on [DEID] for continued symptoms. In the emergency department, 
the patient underwent extensive workup, including a CT scan, 
which did not show any significant findings beyond normal 
post-operative cholecystectomy changes. There were no causes 
identified to explain the patient's symptoms.

The patient was then admitted to the [DEID] Service 
due to the patient's inability to maintain adequate PO intake 
and symptomatic nausea. She was treated symptomatically with 
anti-emetics and she was kept NPO initially. Her diet was 
advanced slowly until the patient was able to tolerate a regular 
diet. 

#NEURO: The patient was alert and oriented throughout 
hospitalization; pain was initially managed with IV pain 
medications while the patient was NPO. The patient was then 
transitioned to a PO pain regime when she was tolerating PO 
intake. The patient's pain was very well controlled.

#CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. 

#PULMONARY: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization. 

#ID: The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs 
of infection, of which there were none. 

#HEME: Patient received BID SQH for DVT prophylaxis, in addition 
to encouraging early ambulation and Venodyne compression 
devices. 

#OTHER:
-WOUND CARE
The patient's epigastric port site had a slight opening and the 
wound was cared for with wet to dry dressings while she was an 
inpatient. The patient was discharged with instructions to apply 
a dry dressing over the site daily.

with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating diet as 
above per oral, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain 
was well controlled. The patient was discharged home without 
services. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up 
instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with 
the discharge plan.

the ileostomy output. Keep yourself well hydrated, if [DEID] notice

#Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome
Patient was restarted on her home xarelto. She was informed that 
it is very important that she continue taking xarelto in the 
setting of her antiphospholipid antibody syndrome. If she needs 
a procedure, she should talk to her doctor about switching to 
lovenox, or admission to hospital to start heparin so that she 
does not go without anticoagulation for too long. She was 
continued on home aspirin 81mg and atorvastatin 80mg. 

#Systemic lupus erythematosus
Patient was previously taking MMF 1500mg BID, hydroxychloroquine 
200mg qd, prednisone 5mg qd, and atovaquone 750mg BID for 
prophylaxis. Upon speaking with her pharmacy, it seems that she 
has not refilled her prescriptions for some time. She should 
follow up with a rheumatologist outpatient for continued 
management. 

#Patent foramen ovale
Her outpatient cardiologist was informed of this admission as 
she missed her TEE appointment due to being in the hospital. She 
should discuss risks/benefits of PFO procedure vs continued 
anticoagulation given that antiphospholipid antibody syndrome 
can cause both arterial and venous thrombosis. Due to the APLS 
syndrome, even if the PFO is closed, it is possible that she 
might still have strokes caused by clots that form in the 
arterial side of her circulation.

She was started on amoxicillin 500mg q8h by a dentist after 
tooth extraction. She finished her course here.

#Chronic anemia
Likely chronic disease. Her Hgb was monitored daily.

#Chronic kidney disease
Likely in setting of lupus erythematosis. Her Cr was monitored 
daily.

#Depression

not filled this prescription at her pharmacy for several months.

Discharge Instructions:
-You can expect to see occasional blood in your urine and to 
possibly experience some urgency and frequency over the next 
month; this may be related to the passage of stone fragments or 
the indwelling ureteral stent.

-The kidney stones were not completely removed AND/or there may 
fragments still in the process of passing.

-You may experience some pain associated with spasm of your 
ureter.; This is normal. Take the narcotic pain medication as 
prescribed if additional pain relief is needed.

-Ureteral stents MUST be removed or exchanged and therefore it 
is IMPERATIVE that you follow-up as directed. 

-Do not lift anything heavier than a phone book (10 pounds) 

-Resume all of your pre-admission medications, except HOLD 
aspirin until you see your urologist in follow-up 

-You may continue to periodically see small amounts of blood in 
your urine--this is normal and will gradually improve

-IBUPROFEN (the ingredient of Advil, Motrin, etc.) may be taken 
even though you may also be taking Tylenol/Acetaminophen. You 
may alternate these medications for pain control. For pain 
control, try TYLENOL FIRST, then ibuprofen, and then take the 
narcotic pain medication as prescribed if additional pain relief 
is needed.

-Ibuprofen should always be taken with food. Please discontinue 
taking and notify your doctor should you develop blood in your 
stool (dark, tarry stools)

-Colace has been prescribed to avoid post surgical constipation 
and constipation related to narcotic pain medication. 
Discontinue if loose stool or diarrhea develops. Colace is a 
stool softener, NOT a laxative, and available over the counter. 
The generic name is DOCUSATE SODIUM. It is recommended that you 
use this medication.

-Do not eat constipating foods for [DEID] weeks, drink plenty of 
fluids to keep hydrated

-No vigorous physical activity or sports until after your next 
procedure

-You have an ostomy appliance over the nephrostomy tube in your 
back. Continue wearing the appliance if you drain urine from the 
tube. If you do not drain urine from the tube, you can contact 
Dr. [DEID] for an alternative dressing over your 
tube site.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. [DEID],

You were admitted to [DEID] and 
underwent placement of a carotid stent in your left carotid 
artery. You tolerated the procedure well and are now ready to be 
discharged. Please follow the instructions below:

MEDICATIONS:
• Continue to take Aspirin 81 mg (enteric coated) once daily 
• Continue to take Plavix (Clopidogrel) 75mg once daily 
• Continue to take Atorvastatin 80 mg once daily
• You may RESTART your Eliquis on [DEID].
• Continue all other medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
• You make take Tylenol or prescribed pain medications for any 
post procedure pain or discomfort

ACTIVITIES:
• When you go home, you may walk and go up and down stairs
• You may shower (let the soapy water run over groin incision, 
rinse and pat dry)
• Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing or 
band aid on that area
• No heavy lifting, pushing or pulling (greater than 5 lbs) for 
1 week (to allow groin puncture to heal)
• After 1 week, you may resume sexual activity
• After 1 week, gradually increase your activities and distance 
walked as you can tolerate
• No driving until you are no longer taking pain medications
• You should NOT have an MRI scan within the first 4 weeks after 
carotid stenting

CALL THE OFFICE FOR: [DEID]
• Changes in vision (loss of vision, blurring, double vision, 
half vision)
• Slurring of speech or difficulty finding correct words to use
• Severe headache or worsening headache not controlled by pain 
medication
• A sudden change in the ability to move, use or feel your arm 
or leg
• Trouble swallowing, breathing, or talking
• Numbness, coldness or pain in lower extremities 
• Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
• New or increased drainage from incision or white, yellow or 
green drainage from incisions
• Bleeding from groin puncture site

FOR SUDDEN, SEVERE BLEEDING OR SWELLING (Groin puncture site)
• Lie down, keep leg straight and have someone apply firm 
pressure to area for 10 minutes. If bleeding stops, call 
vascular office at [DEID]. 
• If bleeding does not stop, call [DEID] for transfer to closest 
Emergency Room.

Activity
•We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, 
or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up appointment.
•You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace once you are symptom free at rest. 
[DEID] try to do too much all at once.
•No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating medication. 
•If you experienced a seizure while admitted, you are NOT 
allowed to drive by law. 
•No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. You 
should avoid contact sports for 6 months. 

Medications
•***Please do NOT take any blood thinning medication (Aspirin, 
Ibuprofen, Plavix, Coumadin) until cleared by the neurosurgeon. 
•***You have been discharged on Keppra (Levetiracetam). This 
medication helps to prevent seizures. Please continue this 
medication as indicated on your discharge instruction. It is 
important that you take this medication consistently and on 
time. 

•You may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort if 
you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.

What You [DEID] Experience:
•You may experience headaches and incisional pain. 
•You may also experience some post-operative swelling around 
your face and eyes. This is normal after surgery and most 
noticeable on the second and third day of surgery. You apply 
ice or a cool or warm washcloth to your eyes to help with the 
swelling. The swelling will be its worse in the morning after 
laying flat from sleeping but decrease when up. 
•You may experience soreness with chewing. This is normal from 
the surgery and will improve with time. Softer foods may be 
easier during this time. 
•Feeling more tired or restlessness is also common.
•Constipation is common. Be sure to drink plenty of fluids and 
eat a high-fiber diet. If you are taking narcotics (prescription 
pain medications), try an over-the-counter stool softener.

Call [DEID] and go to the nearest Emergency Room if you experience

ACUTE ISSUES:
=============
#Acute Hepatitis
Patient presented with epigastric abdominal pain, jaundice, and 
elevated aminotransferases and bilirubin, most consistent with 
hepatocellular pattern of injury vs. obstruction. Of note, the 
patient had consumed half a pint of alcohol before his symptoms 
started but did not report active daily alcohol use. The pattern 
of his transaminases were also not consistent with acute 
alcoholic hepatitis. Other possible causes included viral 
hepatitis, although the patient did not have significant risk 
factors (not sexually active/no new partners, no IVDU). HIV and 
hepatitis serologies were found to be negative. Drug ingestions 
were a possibility, however the patient's serum was negative for 
acetaminophen, and the patient reports no use of any medications 
or drugs of abuse. Acute ischemic hepatitis could also cause 
elevations of LFTs to the thousands, however there was no 
evidence or history of severe hypotension, sepsis, or shock to 
indicate these etiologies. Patient's RUQUS showed patent hepatic 
vasculature, making hepatic congestion (Budd-Chiari) unlikely. 
Wilsons disease was also unlikely although patient is in the 
right age group, given pattern of his transaminases. The patient 
was also tested for viruses such as EBV and CMV, autoimmune 
phenomenon including AMA, AMS A, [DEID], liver kidney microsomal 
antibodies, aldolase, ceruloplasmin. Although some of these 
tests are pending (see transitional issues below) the patient's 
workup has been completely negative to this point.

[DEID]. Thought to be secondary to volume depletion in the setting 
of recent episodes of emesis. Rhabdomyolysis was thought to be 
unlikely as the patient's CK was found to be normal. However 
creatinine did not improve with IV fluids. Patient's FeNa was 
calculated to be 1.1% indicating a more intrinsic etiology. A 
renal ultrasound was ordered to evaluate for possible 
hydronephrosis and signs of obstruction but did not yield any. 
However, it did show hyper echogenicity in the renal cortices 
possibly indicating medical disease. Autoimmune workup was sent 
and nephrology was consulted. With nephrology input, it was 
thought that patient likely has some component of CKD 
contributing to his presentation, though the etiology of it 
remains unclear. He will be followed as an outpatient with 
Nephrology on discharge.

Please call the transplant clinic at [DEID] for fever of 
101 or higher, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, 
inability to tolerate food, fluids or medications, yellowing of 
skin or eyes, increased abdominal pain, incision redness, 
drainage or bleeding, dizziness or weakness, decreased urine 
output or dark, cloudy urine, swelling of abdomen or ankles, 
weight gain of 3 pounds in a day, or any other concerning 
symptoms.

Bring your pill box and list of current medications to every 
clinic visit.

You will need to have labwork drawn on [DEID] at [DEID] 
[DEID] then every [DEID] and [DEID] 

*** On the days you have your labs drawn, do not take your 
Tacrolimus until your labs are drawn. Bring your Tacrolimus with 
you so you may take your medication as soon as your lab work has 
been drawn.

Please continue to measure and record your urine output in the 
"HAT" provided until you are instructed by the transplant clinic 
that you can stop. Bring the record with you to your transplant 
clinic follow up visits

[DEID] drain care: empty and record all output. Bring record of 
output to follow up appointments

Change JP dressing with dry gauze daily and as needed

Follow your medication card, keep it updated with any dosage 
changes, and always bring your card with you to any clinic or 
hospital visits.

You may shower. Allow the water to run over your incision and 
pat area dry. No rubbing, no lotion or powder near the incision. 
You may leave the incision open to the air. The staples are 
removed approximately 3 weeks following your transplant.

No tub baths or swimming

No driving if taking narcotic pain medications

Avoid direct sun exposure. Wear protective clothing and a hat, 
and always wear sunscreen with SPF 30 or higher when you go 
outdoors.

Drink enough fluids to keep your urine light in color. Your 
appetite will return with time. Eat small frequent meals, and 
you may supplement with things like carnation instant breakfast 
or Ensure.

Check your blood sugars and blood pressure at home. Report 
consistently elevated values to the transplant clinic

Do not increase, decrease, stop or start medications without 
consultation with the transplant clinic at [DEID]. There 
are significant drug interactions with anti-rejection 
medications which must be considered in medication management 
following transplant.

Consult transplant binder, and there is always someone on call 
at the transplant clinic with any questions that may arise

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. [DEID],

You were admitted to the Acute Care Surgery service on [DEID] 
with abdominal pain and had imaging done which showed 
diverticulitis with perforation. Diverticulitis is an infection 
in the diverticula of your intestine. Diverticula are tiny 
pouches or weak areas that bulge out from the lining of the wall 
of the intestine (bowel). When these pouches become inflamed or 
infected, it is called diverticulitis. You were managed 
non-operatively with bowel rest, IV fluids, and IV antibiotics. 
You were advanced to a regular diet, which you tolerated. You 
are ambulating and voiding without issue and your pain has 
resolved. Thus, you are now ready to be discharged home to 
finish a two week course of antibiotics. Please follow the 
instructions below: 

Please call your doctor or nurse practitioner or return to the 
Emergency Department for any of the following:
*You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or 
tightness.
*New or worsening cough, shortness of breath, or wheeze.
*If you are vomiting and cannot keep down fluids or your 
medications.
*You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting, diarrhea, 
or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid 
heartbeat, or feeling dizzy or faint when standing.
*You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a 
bowel movement.
*You experience burning when you urinate, have blood in your 
urine, or experience a discharge.
*Your pain in not improving within [DEID] hours or is not gone 
within 24 hours. Call or return immediately if your pain is 
getting worse or changes location or moving to your chest or 
back.
*You have shaking chills, or fever greater than 101.5 degrees 
Fahrenheit or 38 degrees Celsius.
*Any change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern 
you.

Please resume all regular home medications, unless specifically 
advised not to take a particular medication. Also, please take 
any new medications as prescribed.

Please get plenty of rest, continue to ambulate several times 
per day, and drink adequate amounts of fluids. Avoid driving or 
operating heavy machinery while taking pain medications.

Discharge Instructions:

INSTRUCTIONS AFTER ORTHOPAEDIC SURGERY:

- You were in the hospital for orthopedic surgery. It is normal 
to feel tired or "washed out" after surgery, and this feeling 
should improve over the first few days to week. 
- Resume your regular activities as tolerated, but please follow 
your weight bearing precautions strictly at all times.

ACTIVITY AND WEIGHT BEARING:
- Weight bearing as tolerated of the right lower extremity. Full 
hip range of motion

MEDICATIONS:
- Please take all medications as prescribed by your physicians 
at discharge.
- Continue all home medications unless specifically instructed 
to stop by your surgeon.
- Do not drink alcohol, drive a motor vehicle, or operate 
machinery while taking narcotic pain relievers.
- Narcotic pain relievers can cause constipation, so you should 
drink eight 8oz glasses of water daily and take a stool softener 
(colace) to prevent this side effect.

ANTICOAGULATION:
- Please take lovenox daily for 4 weeks

WOUND CARE:
- You may shower. No baths or swimming for at least 4 weeks.
- Any stitches or staples that need to be removed will be taken 
out at your 2-week follow up appointment.
- [DEID] remove your dressing on post operative day 5, and then 
redress with dry sterile gauze as needed for drainage. 

DANGER SIGNS:
Please call your PCP or surgeon's office and/or return to the 
emergency department if you experience any of the following:
- Increasing pain that is not controlled with pain medications
- Increasing redness, swelling, drainage, or other concerning 
changes in your incision
- Persistent or increasing numbness, tingling, or loss of 
sensation
- Fever > 101.4
- Shaking chills
- Chest pain
- Shortness of breath
- Nausea or vomiting with an inability to keep food, liquid, 
medications down
- Any other medical concerns

weeks. Call Dr. [DEID] office to schedule appointment upon 
discharge.
