Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. [DEID] presented to [DEID] holding at [DEID] on [DEID] for 
an open abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) repair. He tolerated the 
procedure well without complications Please see operative note 
for further details. Post-operatively, the patient was taken 
intubated to the cardiovascular intensive care unit (CVICU) for 
close monitoring and further post-operative management. 
On post-operative day one, the patient continued to require 
phenylephrine to support his blood pressure but was otherwise 
stable with adequate urine output and stable labs. He was weaned 
to minimal ventilator settings and thus extubated. Over the next 
couple hours the patient became increasingly hypercarbic 
requiring reintubation. The dilaudid was taken out of his 
epidural and the epidural capped. The following day, on 
post-operative day two, vancomycin and cefepime were started for 
presumed aspiration versus ventilator-associated pneumonia. The 
patient continued to require phenylephrine and was given one 
unit of packed red blood cells in an effort to wean this pressor 
requirement. The epidural was removed as it was unable to be 
used due to ongoing hypotension. The following day, on 
post-operative day three, the patient's sputum cultures were 
growing only gram negative bacteria and thus vancomycin was 
discontinued. Tube feeds were started via patient's orogastric 
tube. Over the next few days, the patient continued to require 
phenylephrine and ventilator support. Two separate attempts at 
diuresis (with a single dose of lasix) resulted in increased 
pressor requirements and thus further attempts were deferred 
until patient is off pressors. 
On post-operative day six ([DEID]), the patient desaturated 
with a turn and was noted to have increased endotracheal 
secretions. A bronchoscopy was performed and thick secretions 
suctioned out. A broncheoalveolar lavage (BAL) was perfomed and 
sent for culture. That evening, the patient was again weaned to 
minimal ventilator settings, passed a spontaneous breathing 
trial, and was thought ready for extubation. Following 
extubation the patient was unable to clear his own secretions 
and approximately 4 hours after extubation the patient had a 
respiratory arrest leading to a pulseless electrical activity 
arrest. The patient received less than five minutes of CPR and 
epinephrine prior to regaining a perfusing rhythm. The patient 
was reintubated and a bronchoscopy was performed, which 
demonstrated thick secretions in his left lung. The following 
day, on post-operative day eight, a tracheostomy was recommended 
to the family given his inability to be safely extubated and to 
clear his secretions. Over the next few days, the patient 
continued to require phenylephrine despite the transfusion of 
two units of packed red blood cells in an attempt to wean it. 
Patient developed a rash that appeared most consistent with a 
drug rash but no new medications were able to be identified. The 
patient's BAL grew Stenotrophomonas and thus Bactrim was started 
(in addition to cefepime).
After several family meetings held during his postoperative 
course, a tracheostomy and PEG was placed by the ACS service, 
Dr. [DEID], at bedside on postoperative day [DEID]. Please 
see operative report for further details. His respiratory status 
has improved, now tolerating trach collar up to 5 hours, and 
CPAP with minimal vent settings. He has been diuresed over the 
past week with a goal of -1L day. In regard to the PEG 
placement, an ideal puncture site was not able to be identified 
easily. A good one-on-one abdominal wall movement was not 
obtained. The decision was made to proceed at 2 cm inferior to 
the costal margin, as that represents the best location. 
Post-procedural CT showed concerns of PEG tube perforating the 
serosa which was ruled out by direct visualtization with 
colonoscopy. He has been on tube feeding via PEG with promote 
with Fiber Full strength at a goal rate of 60ml/hour. His 
abdominal exam has been benign without distention, tenderness.
Of note, during the ICU course the patient developed: 1) 
surgical site infection, opened at bedside and treated with 
wound packing and antibiotics. He has receivied dicloxacillin 
since [DEID] to complete a course until [DEID]. Currently his wound 
has staples and is clean, dry and intact. He will be followed in 
two weeks at Dr. [DEID] for a wound check ; 2) 
catheter related non-occlusive thrombus in the right brachial, 
subclavian and internal jugular veins, which requires three 
months of anticoagulation. He is currently on coumadin at a dose 
of 5 mg daily, with last INR of 2.1 today.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. [DEID],
You were admitted to [DEID] for a schedule open AAA repair. You 
required support with a ventilator given failed attempts to 
extubate you. You had a tracheostomy and PEG placement to 
facilitate your recovery. Over the past weeks, you respiratory 
status has improved and now you are tolerting being off the 
ventilator for short period of times. Meanwhile, you are 
receiving nutrition via your PEG in order to fulffill your 
calorie requirements. In addition, please be aware that you will 
be on coumadin (a blood thinner) for a clot that you have 
developed on your right upper extremity.

WHAT TO LABTEST EXPECT: 1. It is normal to feel weak and tired, this will last for [DEID] 
weeks 
•You should get up out of bed every day and gradually increase 
your activity each day 
•You may walk and you may go up and down stairs 
•Increase your activities as you can tolerate- do not do too 
much right away!

2. It is normal to have incisional and leg swelling:
•Wear loose fitting pants/clothing (this will be less irritating 
to incision) 
•Elevate your legs above the level of your heart with [DEID] 
pillows every [DEID] hours throughout the day and at night
•Avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting without your 
legs elevated

3. It is normal to have a decreased appetite, your appetite will 
return with time 
•You will probably lose your taste for food and lose some weight 

•Eat small frequent meals
•It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
•To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication
•Take all the medications you were taking before surgery, unless 
otherwise directed
•Take one full strength (325mg) enteric coated aspirin daily, 
unless otherwise directed 

ACTIVITIES:
•You should get up every day, get dressed and walk, gradually 
increasing your activity
•Increase your activities as you can tolerate- do not do too 
much right away!
•You may shower (let the soapy water run over incision, rinse 
and pat dry)
•Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing 
over the area

CALL THE OFFICE FOR : [DEID]
•Redness that extends away from your incision
•A sudden increase in pain that is not controlled with pain 
medication
•A sudden change in the ability to move or use your leg or the 
ability to feel your leg
•Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
•Bleeding from incision
•New or increased drainage from incision or white, yellow or 
green drainage from incisions

Ms. [DEID] is a [DEID] with PmedHx significant for hypertension,

#Left Frontal Subacute Infarction: Patient intially presented 
with word finding difficulties and was making paraphasic errors 
(see full exam and HPI above). She was found to have an acute 
ischemic event and was started on heparin gtt. In disucssion 
with the patient, her son, PCP, and the primary team it was 
decided that due to a history of recent hospitalizations for 
falls, continued use of alcohol, age and morbidity that we would 
not pursue aggressive anticoagulation. She was started on ASA 
325. At the time of discharge patient had improved language, was 
no longer making paraphasic errors, could follow complex 
commands, no impairments in reading or writing. She was seen by 
[DEID] who will be performing a home safety evaluation. In addition, 
patietns Hba1C and lipid profile were at goal. As to the 
etiology of her stroke, it was most likely an embolic source, 
although hypoperfusion could not be ruled out. She had an TTE 
which did not show or identify a clot.
.
#Atrial Fibrillation: Patient presented to the hospital with 
Atrial fibrilliation. Per report and history she did not have 
this before, and this was most likely the cause of her stroke. 
On HD2 she went into Afib with RVR, with rates into the 
140's-150s and pressures in the 90's-100's systolic. She was 
given 2.5mg IV metoprolol with good response and started on 
metop tartrate 12.5 BID with adequate rate control with rates in 
the 80's to 90's. She was not deemed a good candidate for 
anticoagulation as above, and therefore will continue ASA 325 as 
an outpatient. Patient has a scheduled follow up appointment 
with her outpatient cardiologist next week.

further behavioral decompensation, legal proceedings for

also started on clonazepam 1mg PO TID. Her mood stabilized and 
she did not have any further behavioral problems during the 
subsequent 9 days of her hospitalization. Clonazepam was tapered 
off. She was continued on Aripiprazole 15mg QHS given patient's 
significant concerns for weight gain and acne with lithium.

consistent with Bipolar I.

hepatology service from clinic with worsening encephalopathy and 
HRS. He was treated for the HRS with octreotide, midodrine and 
albumin. Lactulose and rifaximin were continued for the 
encephalopathy, he did have an episode of increasing confusion, 
but this was managed on the regular surgical floor, he did not 
require an ICU transfer at that time.
He continued to be managed medically. He received paracentesis, 
there was never evidence of SBP and all fluid cultures obtained 
were no growth.

On [DEID] the patient received a liver transplant offer, and 
the patient was taken to the OR with Dr [DEID] for 
deceased donor liver transplant using piggyback
technique with temporary portacaval shunt. A line and dialysis 
catheter in left IJ
for CVVH was placed and he received this continuously during the 
course of the operation. There were no complications reported 
during the surgery, the patient
received 3 liters of crystalloid, 18 units of packed red blood 
cells, 23 units of FFP, 6 platelets, and 1 unit of 
cryoprecipitate. He made 190 mL of urine output and had CVVH 
running throughout the case. He was taken to the SICU is stable 
condition.
In the ensuing 3 hours, the patient had clinical evidence of 
continued bleeding and some hemodynamic instability, so he was 
brought back urgently to the operating room from the intensive 
care unit for control of hemorrhage. In the OR he was found to 
have about 1000 cc of blood and clot around the liver, and this 
was suctioned dry using Cell Saver. Per the operative report, 
there was some ooze from the right
retroperitoneal area around the bare area of the diaphragm, and 
this was closed with a running #1 Prolene stitch. There were 2 
small bleeders on the anterior surface of the vena cava, which 
were controlled with [DEID] Prolene suture ligatures, and some area 
of bleeding behind the vena cava on the left side, which was 
similarly controlled. Another small bleeding vessel near the 
biliary anastomosis on the
recipient side was also controlled with a [DEID] PDS stitch. 
Hemostasis was felt to be achieved at this time, the patient was 
again closed and transferred back to the ICU for further post op 
management. Serial hematocrits were followed, he received one 
additional unit of RBCs post operatively and Hct has been stable 
since.

Patient received routine immunosuppression induction to include 
Solumedrol 500 mg at time of surgery with taper, MMF 1 gram pre 
op with 1 gram BID, prograf was started on the evening of POD 1 
with daily levels and dose adjustments per level. He was 
enrolled in the transplant self med program and did well with 
that teaching.

Patient was extubated on POD 1, and transferred to the regular 
surgical floor on POD 5. He was kept in the ICU so CVVH could be 
continued for solute clearance as well as fluid volume 
management. Urine output was initially approximate 300 cc daily, 
this increased to more than 1.5 liters by POD 5. Electrolytes 
were stable throughout.
The creatinine was 3.9 on day of transplant, he received one HD 
treatment once out of the ICU, the creatinine stabilized in the 
mid 2's, increased to 2.9 for two days, then started trending 
back down and was 2.4 on day of discharge., patient was not 
saving all urine so volume cannot be reported.

LFTs increased initially post op, total bilirubin was as 
elevated as 8.3. This was 0.6 on discharge and all LFTs were WNL

# Acute on chronic HFrEF
On presentation, patient had elevated BNP, reported PND and 
orthopnea, all consistent with acute on chronic HFreF. 
Additionally, CXR showed mild pulmonary edema. On exam, elevated 
JVP to 10 cm, bibasilar crackles. He also reported that he was 
only taking one home medication, ticagrelor 90 mg TID (instead 
of BID). He did not understand his home medication regimen and 
he also had significant financial barriers to receiving his 
medications. Reassuringly, his chest pain was different than 
previous angina symptoms and resolved quickly with IV Lasix. 
Troponins were normal and there were no acute ischemic ECG 
changes (stable Q waves with associated T wave inversions in the 
precordial leads). With diuresis, bibasilar crackles improved 
and his PND/orthopnea resolved. He was transitioned to oral 
furosemide 20 mg prior to discharge, with close follow-up 
scheduled with a [DEID] speaking cardiologist to see in the 
outpatient setting, in [DEID] near the patient's home. 
Medication adherence and the importance of taking these 
medications was stressed with an interpreter. Additionally, the 
importance of a low salt and low fat diet were stressed. He will 
continue to require intensive education and close follow-up in 
order to prevent morbidity associated with his heart failure 
(and CAD). Medications to [DEID] manage his HFrEF that were 
started at discharge include lisinopril 5 mg daily and 
metoprolol succinate 25 mg. 

# CAD s/p CABG and PCI to LAD

medications to prevent progression of his heart disease.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. [DEID],

Thank you for allowing us to participate in your care. As you 
know, you were admitted to the hospital because of the infection 
in your knee. While you were here you underwent several 
surgeries for your infection that unfortunately resulted in an 
amputation of your knee. After your surgeries, you had 
complications including a lung infection and a clot in one of 
your veins. We treated you with antibiotics for your lung 
infection, and blood thinners for your clot. This medication is 
called Warfarin, and you will need to follow up with your 
primary care provider to make sure that the levels of warfarin 
in your body is optimal. You will need to see your primary care 
provider for this within 1 week.

After your surgery your heart had difficulty pumping. We think 
this may be due to your infection. Before you left, you 
underwent an ECHO which took pictures of your heart which showed 
that your heart function has improved. You will still need to 
take two new medications for your heart, lisinopril and 
metoprolol. Please continue to take this medication at home for 
your heart. Please follow up with your cardiologist. 

If you develop any shortness of breath, chest pain, please seek 
medical care. 

Thank you for allowing us to participate in your care. We wish 
you the best of luck in your recovery process. 

1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.
2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills. 
3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect. Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled. These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house. Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.
6. Please keep your wounds clean. You may shower starting five 
(5) days after surgery, but no tub baths or swimming for at 
least four (4) weeks. No dressing is needed if wound continues 
to be non-draining. Any stitches or staples that need to be 
removed will be taken out at first follow up appointment two 
weeks after your surgery.
7. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment in two (2) weeks.
8. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal anti-inflammatory 
medications (NSAIDs such as celebrex, ibuprofen, advil, aleve, 
motrin, etc).
9. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your cpumadin for at least 3 
months to treat and help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood 
clots). If you were taking aspirin prior to your surgery, it is 
OK to continue at your previous dose while taking this 
medication. [DEID] STOCKINGS x 6 WEEKS.
10. WOUND CARE: Please keep your incision clean and dry. It is 
okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four (4) 
week checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound 
each day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air. 
Check wound regularly for signs of infection such as redness or 
thick yellow drainage. Staples/Sutures will be removed AT FIRST 
POST OP APPOINTMENT in three (2) weeks.

===============
SUMMARY
===============
[DEID] with history of MPD/MDS overlap syndrome s/p allo transplant 
with progression to AML s/p reinduction chemotherapy with MEC 
and DLI on [DEID] complicated by GVHD on prednisone and cellcept 
who presents with shortness of breath. CT chest showed a 
pneumonia. Diagnostic testing including bronchoscopy did not 
reveal a definitive source. [DEID] was treated with broad-spectrum 
antibiotics with improvement in dyspnea by discharge.

===============
ACUTE ISSUES
===============
# Pneumonia
# Shortness of Breath
# Cough
Patient had cold symptoms for the 1 month with worsening labored 
breathing and severe fatigue for 1 day prior to admission. 
Recent respiratory viral panel was positive for rhinovirus and 
enterovirus. [DEID] also had a severe dry cough. CT chest on 
admission showed multifocal consolidation mostly in the bases. 
Diagnostic bronchoschopy showed pneumonia, with 2+ PMNs and 1+ 
GPCs in pairs. Nasopharyngeal swab this admission again showed 
Enterovirus and Rhinovirus. The etiology of the pneumonia is 
unclear, potentially viral vs. atypical bacterial vs. typical 
bacterial. [DEID] was treated initially with vanc and cefepime. Vanc 
was stopped was nasal MRSA swab came back negative. [DEID] completed 
1 week of cefepime, as well as 6 days of azithromycin with plans 
to finish the last day at home. His cough was controlled mostly 
with benzonatate. Additionally, as the patient's lisinopril was 
felt to be potentially contributing to his cough, this was 
stopped and [DEID] was started on low-dose valsartan.

===============

===============

There had been some confusion for the patient was to what his 
immunosuppression regimen should be. On admission, [DEID] was 
continued on the 500mg MMF and 10mg prednisone BID. Dr. 
[DEID] the patient and felt that his GVHD was overall 
under good control, and as such MMF was stopped. 10mg BID pred 
was switched to 20mg QAM, with improvement in insomnia. As LFTs 
had downtrended compared to prior, ursodiol was discontinued. 
The patient was additionally noted to have changes c/w chronic 
GVHD, including lichenous changes in the buccal mucosa.

before his bronch.

#AML

Discharge Instructions:
 
Depression: Tips to Help Yourself
 
As your healthcare providers help treat your depression, you can 
also help yourself. Keep in mind that your illness affects you 
emotionally, physically, mentally, and socially. So full 
recovery will take time. Take care of your body and your soul, 
and be patient with yourself as you get better.
 
Be With Others
[DEID] isolate yourself—you’ll only feel worse. Try to be with 
other people. And take part in fun activities when you can. Go 
to a movie, ballgame, religious service, or social event. Talk 
openly with people you can trust. And accept help when it’s 
offered.
 
Keep Your Perspective
Depression can cloud your judgment. So wait until you feel 
better before making major life decisions, such as changing 
jobs, moving, or getting married or divorced.
 
This illness is not your fault. [DEID] blame yourself for your 
depression.
 
Recovering from depression is a process. [DEID] be discouraged if 
it takes some time to feel better.
 
Depression saps your energy and concentration. So you won’t be 
able to do all the things you used to do. Set small goals and do 
what you can.
 
Take Care of Your Body
People with depression often lose the desire to take care of 
themselves. That only makes their problems worse. During 
treatment and afterward, make a point to:
 
-Exercise. It’s a great way to take care of your body. And 
studies have shown that exercise helps fight depression.
 
-Avoid drugs and alcohol. These may ease the pain in the short 
term. But they’ll only make your problems worse in the long run.
 
-Get relief from stress. Ask your healthcare provider for 
relaxation exercises and techniques to help relieve stress.
 
-Eat right. A balanced and healthy diet helps keep your body 
healthy.

underwent rigid bronchoscopy and then flexible bronchoscopy, 
thoracoscopic aspiration of secretions, and bronchoscopic-guided 
tracheal intubation and positioning of the endotracheal tube. He

right thoracotomy and esophagectomy, bronchoscopy with 
aspiration. He was admitted to the SICU immediately 
postoperatively intubated. He was followed with chest films

atelectasis of right lung. He was extubated on [DEID] without 
difficulty, and he was transferred to the floor [DEID]. He had 
excellent pain control with a Bupivicaine, Dilaudid Epidural 
which was converted to PO pain medication on [DEID]. The foley 
catheter was removed at that time and the patient voided without 
difficulty.

Postoperatively, the patient was started on vancomycin, zosyn, 
flagyl, and fluconazole empirically. His wound cultures from 
the native esophagus grew klebsiella which was resistent to a 
number of drugs, and his antibiotic coverage was changed to 
tigecycline and fluconazole. The klebsiella was shown to be 
sensitive to tigecycline, and blood cultures and fungal cultures 
remained negative. The fluconazole was discontinued. Mr. [DEID] 
had a [DEID] fever on postoperative day 2, and blood cultures 
and urine cultures were negative; chest x-ray was negative for 
acute change. On postoperative day 6, his WBC count increased 
from 11.9 to 17.9, and he had a [DEID] fever again at that 
time. Chest CT was negative for evidence of infection or 
pulmonary embolism. His WBC count remained elevated for the 
following two days, during which time sputum cultures and C.diff 
were negative. He remained afebrile for the remainder of his 
postoperative course, and WBC count began to trend downward on 
postoperative day 9.

Three chest tubes were placed at the time of Mr. [DEID] 
operation: an anterior chest tube which was directed 
anteroapically, a posterior tube directed posteroapically, and a 
basilar tube directed at a right angle across the chest. The 
anterior and posterior chest tubes were removed on postoperative 
days 6 and [DEID], respectively, and the basilar chest tube remained 
watersealed to a pneumostat and will be removed at a [DEID] 
clinic appointment. His incisional staples were removed prior 
to discharge from the hospital.

At the time of discharge, Mr. [DEID] was tolerating a regular 
diet, afebrile with stable vital signs, ambulating without 
assistance, voiding without difficulty, and his pain was 
well-controlled with oral pain medications. He was discharged 
to home with the assistance of his roommate to care for his 
chest tube, and with visiting nursing to do his IV antibiotics 
BID for the remainder of the 2 week (total) course of 
tigecycline.

three (3) days after your procedure.

· You will have a small bandage over the site.

· Remove the bandage in 24 hours by soaking it with water 
and gently peeling it off.

· Keep the site clean with soap and water and dry it 
carefully.

· You may use a band-aid if you wish.

· Mild tenderness and bruising at the puncture site (groin).

· Soreness in your arms from the intravenous lines.

· The medication may make you bleed or bruise easily.

· Fatigue is very normal.

When to Call Your Doctor at [DEID] for:

· Severe pain, swelling, redness or drainage from the 
puncture site. 

· Fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit

· Constipation

· Blood in your stool or urine

· Nausea and/or vomiting

 

Call [DEID] and go to the nearest Emergency Room if you experience 
any of the following:

· Sudden numbness or weakness in the face, arm, or leg

· Sudden confusion or trouble speaking or understanding

· Sudden trouble walking, dizziness, or loss of balance or 
coordination

· Sudden severe headaches with no known reason

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the Transplant Surgical Service for 
evaluation and treatment of increasing bilirubin levels. He 
underwent a failed attempt at a tube cholangiogram through his 
two Roux tubes on [DEID]. On [DEID], he successfully had two 
PTBDs placed in the right posterior and right anterior 
intrahepatic ductal systems. The cholangiogram demonstrated a 
stricture in the proximal Roux limb with a smaller stricture 
distal to this. Following this procedure, the patient developed 
fever/rigors even though the tubes were open to gravity. He had 
been given Unasyn periprocedure and his cipro had been stopped. 
With the development of rigors, his antibiotic regimen was 
broadened to vancomycin, zosyn, and cipro. Blood and bile 
cultures grew out multiresistant E. coli and Klebsiella, 
however. He was switched to meropenem with tigecycline added 
later. With more sensitivity information, his regimen was 
changed to colistin alone. A PICC line was placed in 
preparation for antibiotic infusion at home. All told, the 
patient received 2 days of vancomycin, 4 days of tigecycline, 
and 3 days of meropenem. He was started on colistin the day 
prior to discharge.
Meanwhile, the strictures were further evaluated. A CT abdomen 
was obtained, which demonstrated slight distention of the Roux 
limb at the hepaticojejunostomy and a region of focal ectasia of 
the small bowel at the jejunojejunostomy. It was noted that the 
actual stricture within the Roux limb could not be evaluated 
with this static radiological exam. 

Neuro: The patient received tramadol and IV dilaudid with good 
effect and adequate pain control. 

CV: Following his PTBD placement on [DEID], the patient was 
tachycardic to the 140s, but his blood pressure was within 
normal limits. EKG showed sinus tachycardia only, and his hct 
was stable. He was given lopressor 5mg IV once with good 
effect. Thereafter, the patient remained stable from a 
cardiovascular standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored.

Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet and ambulation were encouraged throughout 
hospitalization.

GI/GU/FEN: Periprocedure, the patient was made NPO with IV 
fluids. Diet was advanced both times post-procedure, and this 
was well tolerated. Patient's intake and output were closely 
monitored, and IV fluid was adjusted when necessary. 
Electrolytes were routinely followed. His LFTs and bilirubin 
levels were followed closely throughout his hospitalization. 
When he was admitted, his total bilirubin level was 4.1. The 
peak bilirubin level occurred on [DEID], when it was 5.0. 
Thereafter, it fell steadily and was --- on the day of 
discharge. Similarly, after an initial bump, his LFTs trended 
down following PTBD placement. 

ID: As discussed, the patient grew out panresistent klebsiella 
and multidrug resistent E. coli from his blood and bile. He was 
placed on several different antibiotic regimens as more 
sensitivity data returned, with the final regimen colistin only. 
 The patient's white blood count and fever curves were closely 
watched. His WBC trended up following his procedure, from [DEID].6 to 
a peak of 12.1. His white count remained elevated and was 9.2 
on the day of discharge. Along with the WBC elevation, the 
patient spiked fevers on HD3, 4, and 6. Tmax was 103.5 the day 
following his PTBD placement. By the time of discharge, the 
patient had been afebrile for more than 72 hours. Regarding his 
cultures, initial blood and bile cultures grew pan-resistant E 
coli and Klebsiella. Repeat blood cultures are showing E coli's 
as late as [DEID]. Additional blood cultures are pending at time 
of discharge.

Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined 
routinely; no transfusions were required.

Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin during 
the first portion of his stay. As he was ambulatory, heparin 
was discontinued.

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile x 
more than 72 hours with stable vital signs. The patient was 
tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without 
assistance, and pain was well controlled. His PTBDs were 
draining well, and his lab values were trending down. The 
patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions 
with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge 
plan.

balanced meals throughout the day.

aside from following a Diabetic diet. Poor appetite is not

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. [DEID],

It was a pleasure taking care of you at [DEID]. Please see below 
for discharge instructions:

It was a pleasure taking care of you at [DEID] 
[DEID]. During your hospitalization, you had surgery to 
remove unhealthy tissue on your lower extremity. You tolerated 
the procedure well and are now ready to be discharged from the 
hospital. Please follow the recommendations below to ensure a 
speedy and uneventful recovery.

LOWER EXTREMITY AMPUTATION 
DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS
ACTIVITY 
•You should keep your amputation site elevated and straight 
whenever possible. This will prevent swelling of the stump and 
maintain flexibility in your joint. 
•It is very important that you put no weight or pressure on 
your stump with activity or at rest to allow the wound to heal 
properly. 
•You may use the opposite foot for transfers and pivots, if 
applicable. It will take time to learn to use a walker and 
learn to transfer into and out of a wheelchair. 

MEDICATION
•Before you leave the hospital, you will be given a list of all 
the medicine you should take at home. If a medication that you 
normally take is not on the list or a medication that you do not 
take is on the list please discuss it with the team! 

•You will likely be prescribed narcotic pain medication on 
discharge which can be very constipating. If you take 
narcotics, please also take a stool softener such as Colace. 
If constipation becomes a problem, your pharmacist can suggest 
an additional over the counter laxative. 

•You should take Tylenol [DEID] every 6 hours, as needed for 
pain. If this is not enough, take your prescription narcotic 
pain medication. You should require less pain medication each 
day. Do not take more than a daily total of 3000mg of Tylenol. 
Tylenol is used as an ingredient in some other over-the-counter 
and prescription medications. Be aware of how much Tylenol you 
are taking in a day.

BATHING/SHOWERING: 
•You may shower when you feel strong enough but no tub baths or 
pools until you have permission from your surgeon and the 
incision is fully healed.
•After your shower, gently dry the incision well. Do not rub 
the area. 

WOUND CARE:
•Please keep the wound clean and dry. It is very important that 
there is no pressure on the stump. If there is no drainage, 
you may leave the incision open to air. 
•Your staples/sutures will remain in for at least 4 weeks. At 
your followup appointment, we will see if the incision has 
healed enough to remove the staples. 
•Before you can be fitted for prosthesis (a man-made limb to 
replace the limb that was removed) your incision needs to be 
fully healed. 

CALL THE OFFICE FOR: [DEID]
•Opening, bleeding or drainage or odor from your stump incision 

•Redness, swelling or warmth in your stump.
•Fever greater than 101 degrees, chills, or worsening 
incisional/stump pain 

NO OTHER PROVIDER, EXCEPT YOUR VASCULAR SURGEON, SHOULD 
DETERMINE IF YOUR STAPLES ARE READY TO BE REMOVED FROM YOUR 
STUMP! 
IF THERE ARE ANY QUESTIONS, THE PROVIDER SHOULD CALL THE 
VASCULAR SURGERY OFFICE AT [DEID] TO DISCUSS. THE 
STAPLES WILL BE REMOVED IN THE OFFICE AT YOUR FOLLOWUP 
APPOINTMENT WHEN IT IS DETERMINED BY THE VASCULAR SURGEON THAT 
THE WOUND HAS SUFFICIENTLY HEALED.

abdominal distension and had a CT scan which showed an abdominal 
fluid collection. You were started on antibiotics and we would 
like you to continue to take Cipro and Flagyl for 2 weeks. 
Please take all antibiotics as prescribed. Call or return to 
emergency department for fever greater then 101, severe nausea, 
vomiting dehydration and being unable to maintain oral 
hydration, pain > 24 hrs and unresolved, redness or purulent 
drainage to the incision, shortness of breath or chest pains.

removed and urology were consulted who recommended placement of 
a foley catheter for 5 days and to follow-up with your primary 
care provider or urologist for a voiding trial.

day and follow-up with your endocrinologist for steroid 
tapering. Your blood sugars were elevated during your 
hospitalization and your insulin was adjusted by the 
endocrinologist. Please follow up with your endocrinologist for 
further monitoring of your blood sugars and insulin adjustment.
Please continue to monitor your ileostomy. The most common 
complication from a new ileostomy placement is dehydration. The 
output from the stoma is stool from the small intestine and the 
water content is very high. The stool is no longer passing 
through the large intestine which is where the water from the 
stool is reabsorbed into the body and the stool becomes formed. 
You must measure your ileostomy output for the next few weeks. 
The output from the stoma should not be more than 1200cc or less 
than 500cc. If you find that your output has become too much or 
too little, please call the office for advice. Keep yourself 
well hydrated, if you notice your ileostomy output increasing, 
take in more electrolyte drink such as gatoraide. Please monitor 
yourself for signs and symptoms of dehydration including: 
dizziness (especially upon standing), weakness, dry mouth, 
headache, or fatigue. If you notice these symptoms please call 
the office or return to the emergency room for evaluation if 
these symptoms are severe.

an over the counter medication.
 2) Add oxycodone as needed for increased pain. Aim to wean 
off this medication in 1 week or sooner. This is an example on 
how to wean down:
Take 1 tablet every 3 hours as needed x 1 day,
then 1 tablet every 4 hours as needed x 1 day,
then 1 tablet every 6 hours as needed x 1 day,
then 1 tablet every 8 hours as needed x 2 days, 
then 1 tablet every 12 hours as needed x 1 day,
then 1 tablet every before bedtime as needed x 1 day. 
Then continue with Tylenol for pain.

3. You may have a mild headache, especially on the side of your 
surgery
•Try ibuprofen, acetaminophen, or your discharge pain 
medication
•If headache worsens, is associated with visual changes or 
lasts longer than 2 hours- call vascular surgeon’s office

4. It is normal to feel tired, this will last for [DEID] weeks 
•You should get up out of bed every day and gradually increase 
your activity each day 
•You may walk and you may go up and down stairs 
•Increase your activities as you can tolerate- do not do too 
much right away!

5. It is normal to have a decreased appetite, your appetite will 
return with time 
•You will probably lose your taste for food and lose some 
weight 
•Eat small frequent meals
•It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
•To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
•No driving until post-op visit and you are no longer taking 
pain medications
•No excessive head turning, lifting, pushing or pulling 
(greater than 5 lbs) until your post op visit
•You may shower (no direct spray on incision, let the soapy 
water run over incision, rinse and pat dry)
•Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing 
over the area that is draining, as needed
•Take all the medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
•Take one full strength (325mg) enteric coated aspirin daily, 
unless otherwise directed 
•Call and schedule an appointment to be seen in 2 weeks for 
staple/suture removal

What to report to office:
•Changes in vision (loss of vision, blurring, double vision, 
half vision)
•Slurring of speech or difficulty finding correct words to use
•Severe headache or worsening headache not controlled by pain 
medication
•A sudden change in the ability to move or use your arm or leg 
or the ability to feel your arm or leg
•Trouble swallowing, breathing, or talking
•Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
•Bleeding, new or increased drainage from incision or white, 
yellow or green drainage from incisions

momentum of putting your legs/feet to the ground.

products and supplements that may have “blood-thinning” effects 
(like Fish Oil, Vitamin E, etc.). This will be noted in your 
medication reconciliation.

even though you may also be taking Tylenol/Acetaminophen. You 
may alternate these medications for pain control.

ibuprofen, and then take the narcotic pain medication as 
prescribed if additional pain relief is needed.

taking and notify your doctor should you develop blood in your 
stool (dark, tarry stools)

fluids to keep hydrated

otherwise advised. Light household chores/activity and leisurely 
walking/activity is OK and should be continued. Do NOT be a

pain medication has been prescribed for breakthrough pain [DEID].

from ALL sources

you are cleared by your Urologist in follow-up.

Urologist

bathe

operate dangerous machinery

gentle laxative. These are over-the-counter medications that 
may be “health care spending account reimbursable.” 

-Colace (docusate sodium) may have been prescribed to avoid 
post-surgical constipation or constipation related to use of 
narcotic pain medications. Discontinue if loose stool or 
diarrhea develops. Colace is a stool-softener, NOT a laxative.

-Senokot (or any gentle laxative) may have been prescribed to 
further minimize your risk of constipation.

evaluation of elevated creatinine, elevated INR, and 
intermittent hematuria. The patient was started on aggressive 
fluid resuscitation and labs were monitored serially. On HD2 his

2.1 to 1.5. He showed no evidence of hematuria so his 
anticoagulation was not reversed. The morning of HD3 he 
developed gross hematuria which led to clot retention. Attempts 
were made at hand irrigation at bedside, however, the patient 
was in a lot of pain and minimal clot was expressed. Given the 
patient's discomfort and concern for large amount of organized 
clot in his bladder he was taken to the OR for clot evacuation. 
His labs at that point showed an INR of 3.2 and a creatinine of

operation. The clot was successfully irrigated out of the 
bladder and the patient was started on CBI. Please see dictated 
op note for more details. The patient tolerated the procedure 
well, was extubated in the OR, recovered in the PACU, and 
further recovered on the floor. His INR on HD4 was 2.5, thus he 
was restarted on his home coumadin regimen. His creatinine had 
normalized to 1.1. He was hemodynamically stable with a stable 
HCT during his hospitalization. The patient was maintained on 
CBI until HD5, his urine remained clear following the procedure. 
He complained of some bladder spasms after the procedure and was 
started on BNO suppositories and ditropan. The patient was 
deemed ready for discharge on HD5. He was discharged with his 
Foley with a plan to follow up with Dr. [DEID] on [DEID],

on [DEID] and follow up with his PCP regarding further coumadin 
planning.

Discharge Instructions:
Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery
Endovascular Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA) Discharge 
Instructions

Medications:
•Take Aspirin 81mg (enteric coated) once daily 
•Do not stop Aspirin unless your Vascular Surgeon instructs you 
to do so. 
•Continue all other medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
•You make take Tylenol or prescribed pain medications for any 
post procedure pain or discomfort

What to expect when you go home:
It is normal to have slight swelling of the legs:
•Elevate your leg above the level of your heart (use [DEID] 
pillows or a recliner) every [DEID] hours throughout the day and at 
night
•Avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting without your 
legs elevated
It is normal to feel tired and have a decreased appetite, your 
appetite will return with time 
•Drink plenty of fluids and eat small frequent meals
•It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
•To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
•When you go home, you may walk and go up and down stairs
•You may shower (let the soapy water run over groin incision, 
rinse and pat dry)
•Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing or 
band aid over the area that is draining, as needed
•No heavy lifting, pushing or pulling (greater than 5 lbs) for 
1 week (to allow groin puncture to heal)
•After 1 week, you may resume sexual activity
•After 1 week, gradually increase your activities and distance 
walked as you can tolerate
•No driving until you are no longer taking pain medications
•Call and schedule an appointment to be seen in [DEID] weeks for 
post procedure check and CTA 

What to report to office:
•Numbness, coldness or pain in lower extremities 
•Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
•New or increased drainage from incision or white, yellow or 
green drainage from incisions
•Bleeding from groin puncture site

SUDDEN, SEVERE BLEEDING OR SWELLING (Groin puncture site or 
incision)
•Lie down, keep leg straight and have someone apply firm 
pressure to area for 10 minutes. If bleeding stops, call 
vascular office. If bleeding does not stop, call [DEID] for 
transfer to closest Emergency Room.

cultures returned showing highly resistant E. coli (likely the

breast pump was placed at bedside.

without any changes.

hospital. We wish you the best of health.

Do not drink alcohol or take illegal drugs.

Sleep Hygiene

The most common cause of insomnia is a change in your daily 
routine. For example, traveling, change in work hours, 
disruption of other behaviors (eating, exercise, leisure, etc.), 
and relationship conflicts can all cause sleep problems. Paying 
attention to good sleep hygiene is the most important thing you 
can do to maintain good sleep.

LABTEST Do: 1. Go to bed at the same time each day.
2. Get up from bed at the same time each day.
3. Get regular exercise each day, preferably in the morning. 
There is good evidence that regular exercise improves restful 
sleep. This includes stretching and aerobic exercise.
4. Get regular exposure to outdoor or bright lights, especially 
in the late afternoon.
5. Keep the temperature in your bedroom comfortable.
6. Keep the bedroom quiet when sleeping.
7. Keep the bedroom dark enough to facilitate sleep.
8. Use your bed only for sleep and sex.
9. Take medications as directed. It is helpful to take 
prescribed sleeping pills 1 hour before bedtime, so they are 
causing drowsiness when you lie down, or 10 hours before getting 
up, to avoid daytime drowsiness.
10. Use a relaxation exercise just before going to sleep.
o Muscle relaxation, imagery, massage, warm bath, etc.
11. Keep your feet and hands warm. Wear warm socks and/or 
mittens or gloves to bed.

Don'LABTEST t: 1. Exercise just before going to bed.
2. Engage in stimulating activity just before bed, such as 
playing a competitive game, watching an exciting program on 
television or movie, or having an important discussion with a 
loved one.
3. Have caffeine in the evening (coffee, many teas, chocolate, 
sodas, etc.) .
4. Read or watch television in bed.
5. Use alcohol to help you sleep.
6. Go to bed too hungry or too full.
7. Take another person's sleeping pills.
8. Take over-the-counter sleeping pills, without your doctor's 
knowledge. Tolerance can develop rapidly with these medications. 
Diphenhydramine (an ingredient commonly found in 
over-the-counter sleep meds) can have serious side effects for 
elderly patients.
9. Take daytime naps.
10. Command yourself to go to sleep. This only makes your mind 
and body more alert.

If you lie in bed awake for more than [DEID] minutes, get up, go 
to a different room (or different part of the bedroom), 
participate in a quiet activity (e.g. non-excitable reading or 
television), and then return to bed when you feel sleepy. Do 
this as many times during the night as needed.

Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery
Lower Extremity Angioplasty/Stent Discharge Instructions

Medications:
•Take Aspirin 325mg (enteric coated) once daily 
•If instructed, take Plavix (Clopidogrel) 75mg once daily
•Continue all other medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
•You make take Tylenol or prescribed pain medications for any 
post procedure pain or discomfort

What to expect when you go home:
It is normal to have slight swelling of the legs:
•Elevate your leg above the level of your heart (use [DEID] 
pillows or a recliner) every [DEID] hours throughout the day and at 
night
•Avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting without your 
legs elevated
•It is normal to feel tired and have a decreased appetite, your 
appetite will return with time 
•Drink plenty of fluids and eat small frequent meals
•It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
•To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
•When you go home, you may walk and go up and down stairs
•You may shower (let the soapy water run over groin incision, 
rinse and pat dry)
•Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing or 
band aid over the area that is draining, as needed
•No heavy lifting, pushing or pulling (greater than 5 lbs) for 
1 week (to allow groin puncture to heal)
•After 1 week, you may resume sexual activity
•After 1 week, gradually increase your activities and distance 
walked as you can tolerate
•No driving until you are no longer taking pain medications
•Call and schedule an appointment to be seen in [DEID] weeks for 
post procedure check and ultrasound 

What to report to office:
•Numbness, coldness or pain in lower extremities 
•Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
•New or increased drainage from incision or white, yellow or 
green drainage from incisions
•Bleeding from groin puncture site

SUDDEN, SEVERE BLEEDING OR SWELLING (Groin puncture site)
•Lie down, keep leg straight and have someone apply firm 
pressure to area for 10 minutes. If bleeding stops, call 
vascular office [DEID]. If bleeding does not stop, call 
[DEID] for transfer to closest Emergency Room.

[DEID] year old female multiple medical comorbidities including 
history of DVT/PE on Coumadin presenting to [DEID] after being 
found down with altered mental status. In the emergency

chest, abdomen and pelvis by CT as well as x-ray of the 
bilateral knees notable for extraperitoneal pelvic hematoma, 
left hip fracture, and multiple bilateral comminuted pubic rami 
fractures. Patient had decreased responsiveness, with oxygen 
desaturation at [DEID] requiring 3 LPM NC, BP 94/46. The patient 
was admitted to the ICU and given 1 unit of PRBC and vitamin K. 
CTA showed possible active extravasation, not definitively seen. 
 

The patient's hematocrit remained stable and cardiovascular

floor on HD2. Orthopedics had been consulted and recommended 
non-operative management at this time, weight bearing as 
tolerated. Geriatrics was consulted due to altered mental status 
and polypharmacy. Adjustments were made, trazadone was stopped 
and replaced with ramelteon. Hematology was consulted for 
recommendations on anticoagulation due to history of DVT/PE. 
They recommended lovenox for 2 weeks. On HD8, the patient was 
complaining of burning with urination. A urinalysis was 
positive, and the patient was started on 7 day course of 
macrobid for UTI.

the hospitalization. They recommended rehab once medically 
clear. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, 
afebrile with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a 
regular diet, out of bed with assist, voiding without 
assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient was 
discharged to rehab. The patient received discharge teaching and 
follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and 
agreement with the discharge plan. She had follow-up scheduled 
with Orthopedics.

Brief Hospital Course:
[DEID] yo LHW with h/o POTS, Ehlers Danlos, and gastroparesis s/p 
gastric pacemaker presents with 3 months of left sided spasm, 
twitching and weakness. 

Neurologic exam was notable for give-way weakness on the left 
side, but the patient was able to exert full strength with first 
initial effort. She had a left hand tremor with irregular 
frequency, that waxed and waned and appeared to be 
position-related. 
Her description of the symptoms as visual rippling with spasm 
and weakness did not sound typical for any particular nerve or 
muscle problem. The differential diagnosis was therefore broad, and she 
received an extensive workup to facilitate expediated assessment 
of these symptoms which are highly concerning to her. 

CT of the head and neck, CTA and CT of the C-spine were all 
normal. There was an incidental finding of a thyroid nodule that 
does require a follow up ultrasound per her PCP's judgement. 
She could not undergo an MRI due to gastric pacemaker, thus an 
EEG was done to evaluate for seizure activity as well as any 
focal area of abnormality too subtle to be detected on CT. She 
was monitored with continuous bedside video EEG for 24 hours. 
She did have a push button event when she was experiencing 
twitching and spasms, but there was no electrical correlate. She 
had no seizures or other abnormal activity on the 24 hour EEG. 
EMG was done to evaluate for myopathy, and this was normal 
except for poor effort in muscle recruitment. This further 
supported that she did in fact have full strength, and 
correlated with the physical exam. 
LP was done, and cell count, gram stain, and TP/glucose were all 
normal. Oligoclonal bands to rule out MS are pending. 

In conclusion, it was felt that patient's symptoms had no 
anatomical correlate and did not represent a primary neurologic 
diagnosis. She will continue to follow with her outpatient 
providers.

The patient was treated symptomically with flexeril and 
skelaxin. She may benefit from scheduled antispasm medication, 
and was discharged on skelaxin tid. She will follow up with Dr. 
[DEID] to review the rest of her testing (ie MS profile). 
It is unlikely that these symptoms are related to her autonomic 
failure. She will also continue to follow with her geneticist at 
[DEID].

Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery
Carotid Endarterectomy Surgery Discharge Instructions

What to expect when you go LABTEST home: 1. Surgical Incision:
•It is normal to have some swelling and feel a firm ridge along 
the incision
•Your incision may be slightly red and raised, it may feel 
irritated from the staples

2. You may have a sore throat and/or mild hoarseness
•Try warm tea, throat lozenges or cool/cold beverages 

3. You may have a mild headache, especially on the side of your 
surgery
•Try ibuprofen, acetaminophen, or your discharge pain 
medication
•If headache worsens, is associated with visual changes or 
lasts longer than 2 hours- call vascular surgeon’s office

4. It is normal to feel tired, this will last for [DEID] weeks 
•You should get up out of bed every day and gradually increase 
your activity each day 
•You may walk and you may go up and down stairs 
•Increase your activities as you can tolerate- do not do too 
much right away!

5. It is normal to have a decreased appetite, your appetite will 
return with time 
•You will probably lose your taste for food and lose some 
weight 
•Eat small frequent meals
•It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
•To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
•No driving until post-op visit and you are no longer taking 
pain medications
•No excessive head turning, lifting, pushing or pulling 
(greater than 5 lbs) until your post op visit
•You may shower (no direct spray on incision, let the soapy 
water run over incision, rinse and pat dry)
•Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing 
over the area that is draining, as needed
•Take all the medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
•Take one full strength (325mg) enteric coated aspirin daily, 
unless otherwise directed 
•Call and schedule an appointment to be seen in 2 weeks for 
staple/suture removal

What to report to office:
•Changes in vision (loss of vision, blurring, double vision, 
half vision)
•Slurring of speech or difficulty finding correct words to use
•Severe headache or worsening headache not controlled by pain 
medication
•A sudden change in the ability to move or use your arm or leg 
or the ability to feel your arm or leg
•Trouble swallowing, breathing, or talking
•Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
•Bleeding, new or increased drainage from incision or white, 
yellow or green drainage from incisions

Discharge Instructions:
* You were admitted to the hospital for placement of a feeding 
tube so that you could be supplemented during your chemo and 
radiation therapy and maintain your caloric needs.
* Check the J tube site daily to assess for redness or drainage.
* The [DEID] will help teach you the proper way to care for the 
tube, how to flush it to maintain patency and how to administer 
tube feedings. There are also some instructions below.
* You should continue to eat and drink on your own as best you 
can.
* You will follow up with [DEID] RD in the clinic who will 
assess your caloric needs and follow your intake as well as 
adjust your tube feedings.
* Shower daily, no tub baths. The J tube can get wet, just don't 
rub it excessively and pat dry following your shower.
* Your discomfort following tube placement should resolve within 
a week or two. Take pain medication as needed but keep in mind 
that narcotic pain meds can cause constipation therefore take a 
stool softener and even a laxative if needed.
* You will be able to have the tube feedings over a 12 hour 
period for your convenience as opposed to 24 hours a day. We 
just need to be sure that you can tolerate the volume and the 
dietician will help you plan that out. See schedule below.
* If you develop any redness or drainage around the tube or any 
new symptoms that concern you call Dr. [DEID] at 
[DEID].

CARING FOR YOUR J-TUBE

Gather supplies: wet wash cloth or gauze pad, cotton swab, drain
sponge (if needed), gauze sponge, tape 

1) Wash your hands with soap and water. Dry well.
2) Remove any old dressing
3) Wet soft wash cloth or gauze pad with warm soapy water.
4) Clean carefully the skin around the feeding tube. Be careful
not to pull the tube out. 
5) Pat dry.
6) If disc or bolster present on feeding tube, dip a cotton swab
in warm water and gently clean under disc or bolster. 
7) Use additional cotton swab to dry area.
8) Note any redness, swelling, bleeding, or leakage around the
opening. Call a member of your current health care team if you
should see signs of any of the above.
9) Apply new drain sponge dressing under bolster if still having
drainage from stoma site. After stoma site has healed no 
dressing
should be needed.
10) Apply gauze over drain sponge and secure with tape.
11) Wash hands with soap and water. Dry well.

USING YOUR TUBE FOR PUMP FEEDING

Gather supplies: formula, feeding bag, IV pole, 60 ml syringe,
cup of water

1) Wash your hands with soap and water, dry well.
2) Write date and time on bag.
3) Attach feeding set to pump and close the clamp on the tubing.
4) Pour desired amount of feeding into bag.
5) Draw up 60 ml water in syringe, unclamp feeding tube.
6) Push 60ml of water slowly through the tube.
7) Re-clamp tube and remove syringe.
8) Attach pump tubing to feeding tube and unclamp.
9) Start pump at desired rate.
10) If getting into bed, make sure head of bed is elevated 30
degrees.
11) When feeding completed, flush tube with 60 ml water.
12) Take down feeding bag and throw away. 
13) Store any opened containers of formula in the refrigerator.
Label with date and time and use within 24 hours.
14) Any pump issues call your Home infusion/Tube Feeding
Company.

***Flush your j-tube with [DEID] mL of water prior to and after
tube feeding infusion to keep tube clean.

***You need approximately 1 liter (or 4 8oz cups) of fluid in
addition to your goal tube feeds to meet hydration needs. If 
you
are unable to consume fluids by mouth, flush j-tube [DEID] times 
per
day w/ 180 mL (or 3 syringes) of water to help meet your
hydration needs.

***Monitor closely for signs and symptoms of dehydration (dark
colored urine, dizziness w/ standing, constipation, dry mouth)
and increase po fluid intake and/or free water flushes via 
j-tube
(up to 180 mL at a time) as needed.

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted with chest pain and shortness of breath. This 
was caused by blockages in the vessels supplying your heart. You 
did not have a heart attack. However these blockages were severe 
enough to require stenting, which cleared the blockages. 

However, the blockages and your rapid heart rate have combined 
to decrease the pumping ability of your heart. This caused fluid 
to build up in you lungs and legs, causing swelling of your 
legs. You were given a drug called Lasix to pull this fluid off 
and will continue on a low dose to keep this fluid off. 

We have also altered your medications significantly. Please take 
all your medications as prescribed. It is especially important 
that you take your aspirin and Plavix everyday. Please be sure 
to make all of your follow up appointments. 

You also had some foot pain which is consistent with some mild 
osteoarthritis. Please continue to take tylenol as needed.

You also had a mild infection of the skin on your right wrist, 
likely caused by an IV placed there. You were started on an 
antibiotic called Keflex that you will take for approximately 
one week. Also, it appears you have some external yeast growing 
in the folds of your groin. You will be given a prescription for 
miconazole powder to treat this. 

You must now eat a low sodium diet to avoid another build up of 
fluid. This includes canned foods, canned soups, processed meats 
and cheeses, and crackers. Please look for the sodium content on 
labels. You must eat less than 2000mg or 2grams per day. 
Please call your doctor or return to the emergency room if you 
experience chest pain, shortness of breath, fevers, chills, 
bleeding, nausea, vomitting or any other symptom that concerns 
you.

Discharge Instructions:
Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery
Endovascular Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA) Discharge 
Instructions

Medications:
•Take Aspirin 325mg (enteric coated) once daily 
•Do not stop Aspirin unless your Vascular Surgeon instructs you 
to do so. 
•Continue all other medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
•You make take Tylenol or prescribed pain medications for any 
post procedure pain or discomfort

What to expect when you go home:
It is normal to have slight swelling of the legs:
•Elevate your leg above the level of your heart (use [DEID] 
pillows or a recliner) every [DEID] hours throughout the day and at 
night
•Avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting without your 
legs elevated
It is normal to feel tired and have a decreased appetite, your 
appetite will return with time 
•Drink plenty of fluids and eat small frequent meals
•It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
•To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
•When you go home, you may walk and go up and down stairs
•You may shower (let the soapy water run over groin incision, 
rinse and pat dry)
•Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing or 
band aid over the area that is draining, as needed
•No heavy lifting, pushing or pulling (greater than 5 lbs) for 
1 week (to allow groin puncture to heal)
•After 1 week, you may resume sexual activity
•After 1 week, gradually increase your activities and distance 
walked as you can tolerate
•No driving until you are no longer taking pain medications
•Call and schedule an appointment to be seen in [DEID] weeks for 
post procedure check and CTA 

What to report to office:
•Numbness, coldness or pain in lower extremities 
•Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
•New or increased drainage from incision or white, yellow or 
green drainage from incisions
•Bleeding from groin puncture site

SUDDEN, SEVERE BLEEDING OR SWELLING (Groin puncture site or 
incision)
•Lie down, keep leg straight and have someone apply firm 
pressure to area for 10 minutes. If bleeding stops, call 
vascular office. If bleeding does not stop, call [DEID] for 
transfer to closest Emergency Room.

You were admitted to the gynecology service after your 
procedure. You have recovered well and the team believes you are

* Take your medications as prescribed.
* Do not drive while taking narcotics.
* Take a stool softener such as colace while taking narcotics to 
prevent constipation.
* Do not combine narcotic and sedative medications or alcohol.
* Do not take more than 4000mg acetaminophen (APAP) in 24 hrs.
* No strenuous activity until your post-op appointment.
* Nothing in the vagina (no tampons, no douching, no sex) for 3 
months.
* No heavy lifting of objects >10 lbs for 6 weeks.
* You may eat a regular diet.
* You may walk up and down stairs.

Incision care:
* You may shower and allow soapy water to run over incision; no 
scrubbing of incision. No tub baths for 6 weeks.
* Since you have steri-strips, leave them on. They will fall off 
on their own or be removed during your followup visit.
* If you have staples, they will be removed at your follow-up 
visit.

Call your doctor for:
* fever > 100.4F
* severe abdominal pain
* difficulty urinating
* vaginal bleeding requiring >1 pad/hr
* abnormal vaginal discharge
* redness or drainage from incision
* nausea/vomiting where you are unable to keep down fluids/food 
or your medication

* Incorporate 20 to 35 grams of fiber into your daily diet to 
maintain normal bowel function. Examples of high fiber foods

vegetables, Dried fruits such as dried apricots and prunes, 
Legumes, Nuts/seeds. 
* Take Colace stool softener [DEID] times daily.
* Use Dulcolax suppository daily as needed.
* Take Miralax laxative powder daily as needed. 
* Stop constipation medications if you are having loose stools 
or diarrhea.

The patient presented to pre-op on [DEID]. patient was 
evaluated by anaesthesia and taken to the operating room for 
Laparoscopic total proctocolectomy with end
ileostomy. There were no adverse events in the operating room; 
please see the operative note for details. patient was extubated, 
taken to the PACU until stable, then transferred to the floor 
for continued care. 

Neuro: The patient received toradol, IV tylenol, and a dilaudid 
PCA with good effect and adequate pain control. When tolerating 
oral intake, the patient was transition ed to PO tylenol and 
oxycodone, with oxycontin added for adequate pain control. 
CV: Patient had soft blood pressures post-operatively the 
required a 500cc bolus on POD 1 for a systolic blood pressure of 
86mmHg that responded appropriately. The patient had a episode 
of tachycardia to the 130s on POD3 associated with malaise, 
diaphoresis and discomfort but without hypotension that resolved 
after administration of two 500cc boluses. Thereafter patient 
remained stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; vital signs 
were routinely monitored.
Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization.
GI/GU/FEN: Post-operatively, the patient was made NPO with IV 
fluids. Diet was advanced from sips to clears on POD 1, which 
was well tolerated. Patient began to put stool and gas out into 
ostomy bag and was advanced to regular diet on POD 2, IV fluids 
were stopped, she was started on PO medications, her JP drain 
was discontinued, and her Foley came out that night at midnight, 
and sh voided without incident. Patient's intake and output 
were closely monitored, and IV fluid was adjusted when 
necessary. Patient had 750mL of output on POD 3 and had over 
800ml by mid day POD 4 and recieved 2mg of loperamide before 
discharge with instructions for titrating output to for a goal 
of around 1L a day. Electrolytes were routinely followed, and 
repleted when necessary.
ID: The patient's white blood count and fever curves were 
closely watched for signs of infection. On POD 3 the patient 
had an episode of tachycardia, felt uncomfortable, and was 
clammy and labs, urine analysis, and blood cultures were sent. 
The UA was contaminated but was nitrite negative and few 
bacteria, and blood cultures have had no growth to date. 
However, her white count was elevated to 31K. She felt 
subjectively better later that same day, was afebrile, and 
tachycardia resolved. She continued to feel better into POD4 
and her white count came down to 11K. Her wounds rea mined 
clean, dry, and intact and without signs of infection throughout 
her stay. 
Endocrine: The patient's blood sugar was monitored throughout 
his stay; insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly.
Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined 
routinely; no transfusions were required.
Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and 
venodyne boots were used during this stay; was encouraged to get 
up and ambulate as early as possible.

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled. The patient received discharge teaching, was set up 
with a home visiting nurse, and follow-up instructions were 
given with understanding verbalized and agreement with the 
discharge plan.

[DEID] have a new ileostomy. The most common complication from a 
new ileostomy placement is dehydration. The output from the 
stoma is stool from the small intestine and the water content is 
very high. The stool is no longer passing through the large 
intestine which is where the water from the stool is reabsorbed 
into the body and the stool becomes formed. [DEID] must measure 
your ileostomy output for the next few weeks. The output from 
the stoma should not be more than 1200cc or less than 500cc. If 
[DEID] find that your output has become too much or too little, 
please call the office for advice. The office nurse or nurse 
practitioner can recommend medications to increase or slow the 
ileostomy output. Keep yourself well hydrated, if [DEID] notice 
your ileostomy output increasing, take in more electrolyte drink 
such as gatoraide. Please monitor yourself for signs and 
symptoms of dehydration including: dizziness (especially upon 
standing), weakness, dry mouth, headache, or fatigue. If [DEID] 
notice these symptoms please call the office or return to the 
emergency room for evaluation if these symptoms are severe. [DEID] 
may eat a mosified regular diet with your new ileostomy. 

Please monitor the appearance of the ostomy and stoma and care 
for it as instructed by the wound/ostomy nurses. [DEID] stoma 
(intestine that protrudes outside of your abdomen) should be 
beefy red or pink, it may ooze small amounts of blood at times

the ostomy site should be kept clean and intact. Monitor the 
skin around the stoma for buldging or signs of infection listed 
above. Please care for the ostomy as [DEID] have been instructed by 
the wound/ostomy nurses. [DEID] will be able to make an appointment 
with the ostomy nurse in the clinic [DEID] days after surgery. 
[DEID] will have a visiting nurse at home for the next few weeks

[DEID] have [DEID] laparoscopic surgical incisions on your abdomen

[DEID] monitor these areas for signs and symptoms of infection 
including: increasing redness of the incision lines, 
white/gree/yellow/malodorous drainage, increased pain at the 
incision, increased warmth of the skin at the incision, or 
swelling of the area. Please call the office if [DEID] develop any

Discharge Instructions:
Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery
Lower Extremity Angioplasty/Stent Discharge Instructions

Medications:
•If instructed, take Aspirin 325mg (enteric coated) once daily 
•If instructed, take Plavix (Clopidogrel) 75mg once daily
•Continue all other medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
•You make take Tylenol or prescribed pain medications for any 
post procedure pain or discomfort

What to expect when you go home:
It is normal to have slight swelling of the legs:
•Elevate your leg above the level of your heart (use [DEID] 
pillows or a recliner) every [DEID] hours throughout the day and at 
night
•Avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting without your 
legs elevated
•It is normal to feel tired and have a decreased appetite, your 
appetite will return with time 
•Drink plenty of fluids and eat small frequent meals
•It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
•To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
•When you go home, you may walk and go up and down stairs
•You may shower (let the soapy water run over groin incision, 
rinse and pat dry)
•Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing or 
band aid over the area that is draining, as needed
•No heavy lifting, pushing or pulling (greater than 5 lbs) for 
1 week (to allow groin puncture to heal)
•After 1 week, you may resume sexual activity
•After 1 week, gradually increase your activities and distance 
walked as you can tolerate
•No driving until you are no longer taking pain medications
•Call and schedule an appointment to be seen in [DEID] weeks for 
post procedure check and ultrasound 

What to report to office:
•Numbness, coldness or pain in lower extremities 
•Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
•New or increased drainage from incision or white, yellow or 
green drainage from incisions
•Bleeding from groin puncture site

SUDDEN, SEVERE BLEEDING OR SWELLING (Groin puncture site)
•Lie down, keep leg straight and have someone apply firm 
pressure to area for 10 minutes. If bleeding stops, call 
vascular office [DEID]. If bleeding does not stop, call 
[DEID] for transfer to closest Emergency Room.

for starting infliximab (Remicade) as an outpatient, and patient's 
PPD was noted to have a 5mm induration. Given this result, patient 
was started on isoniazid [DEID] mg PO daily x9 months and 
pyridoxine 50 mg PO daily x9 months, which he refused as an 
inpatient. patient was discharged with prescriptions for these 
medications.
.
# Pain control: patient was initially provided hydromorphone 2mg Q4H 
PRN and standing acetaminophen 1g Q6H for pain control, given 
concern for slowing bowel motility, risk for overdose, as well 
as patient's history of substance abuse and past tampering with his 
narcotics PCA. As his hospital course progressed, patient increased 
his requests for higher doses of hydromorphone, and requested 
that diazepam be added for pain control.
.
Clinical exam findings, however, did not support a GI or 
physiologic basis for his reported [DEID] pain. patient was noted to 
be distractable on abdominal exam, and was not tachycardic, 
tachypneic, or hypertensive during his admission. Flexible 
sigmoidscopy as well as CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast 
demonstrated no obvious GI basis for patient's reported persistent 
[DEID] LUQ pain. Of note, patient was observed to refrain from asking 
for pain meds when [DEID] particular RN was on shift, but then to 
increase his demands from other RNs. Because of this 
constellation of findings, patient's reported pain was not felt to be 
physiologic, and hydromorphone IV was therefore gradually 
tapered and changed to PO meds. During this taper, patient was noted 
to be comfortable and in no distress. patient was not discharged on 
pain meds.
.
# Adjustment disorder with disturbance of mood and conduct: patient 
threatened to kill himself and his roommate, as well as to harm 
staff members, when informed that the hydromorphone IV would not 
be increased and would be gradually tapered. patient was therefore 
placed on suicide/homicide precautions. Psychiatry was 
consulted and later cleared patient for discharge. patient was started on 
quetiapine 25mg QAM and QPM, with quetiapine 75mg QHS, and was 
referred for outpatient psychiatry services.
.
# GERD: patient continued on pantoprazole 40 mg daily.
